question_text: The following concentration were obtained for the formation of NH 3 from N 2 and H 2 at equilibrium at 500 K. [N 2 ] = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 M. [H 2 ] = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 M and [NH 3 ] = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 M. Calculate the equilibrium constant.
question_html: The following concentration were obtained for the formation of NH3 from N2 and H2 at equilibrium at 500 K. [N2] = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2M. [H2] = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2M and [NH3] = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2M. Calculate the equilibrium constant.
solution_text: For the given reaction : N 2 (g) + 3H 2 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH 3 (g); The equilibrium constant can be written as follows: {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{{{[N{H_3}]}^2}}}{{[{N_2}]{{[{H_2}]}^3}}}{/tex} Given [N 2 ] = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 M [H 2 ] = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 M [NH 3 ] = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 M Put the given values in above expression, we get. K c {tex} = \frac{{{{[1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}M]}^2}}}{{[1.5 \times {{10}^{-2}}M]{{[3.0 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}M]}^3}}}{/tex} = 0.106 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 = 1.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 M
solution_html:
For the given reaction : N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3(g); The equilibrium constant can be written as follows:
{tex}{K_c} = \frac{{{{[N{H_3}]}^2}}}{{[{N_2}]{{[{H_2}]}^3}}}{/tex}  
Given
[N2] = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2M
[H2] = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M
[NH3] = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M
Put the given values in above expression, we get.
Kc{tex} = \frac{{{{[1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}M]}^2}}}{{[1.5 \times {{10}^{-2}}M]{{[3.0 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}M]}^3}}}{/tex}
= 0.106 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 = 1.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 103M

subject: Chemistry
question_text: At equilibrium, the concentrations of N 2 = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 M, O 2 = 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 M and NO = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 M in a sealed vessel at 800K. What will be K c for the reaction {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}){/tex}
question_html:
At equilibrium, the concentrations of N2 = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3M, O2 = 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3M and NO = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3M in a sealed vessel at 800K. What will be Kc for the reaction
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}){/tex}
solution_text: For the reaction equilibrium constant, K c can be written as, {tex}K_{c}=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\right)^{2}}{\left(3.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\right)\left(4.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\right)}{/tex} = 0.622
solution_html:
For the reaction equilibrium constant, Kc can be written as,
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left(2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\right)^{2}}{\left(3.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\right)\left(4.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\right)}{/tex}
= 0.622

subject: Chemistry
question_text: PCl 5 , PCl 3, and Cl 2 are at equilibrium at 500 K and having a concentration 1.59M PCl 3 , 1.59M Cl 2, and 1.41 M PCl 5 . Calculate K c for the reaction, PCl 5 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCl 3 + Cl 2
question_html:
PCl5, PCl3, and Cl2 are at equilibrium at 500 K and having a concentration 1.59M PCl3, 1.59M Cl2, and 1.41 M PCl5.
Calculate Kc for the reaction, PCl5 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCl3 + Cl2
solution_text: The equilibrium constant k c for the above reaction can be written as {tex}K_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]}=\frac{(1.59)^{2}}{(1.41)}{/tex} = 1.79
solution_html:
The equilibrium constant kc for the above reaction can be written as
{tex}K_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]}=\frac{(1.59)^{2}}{(1.41)}{/tex} = 1.79

question_text: The value of K c = 4.24 at 800K for the reaction, CO(g) + H 2 O(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CO 2 (g) + H 2 (g) Calculate equilibrium concentrations of CO 2 , H 2 , CO, and H 2 O at 800 K, if only CO and H 2 O are present initially at concentrations of 0.10M each.
question_html:
The value of Kc = 4.24 at 800K for the reaction,
CO(g) + H2O(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CO2(g) + H2(g)
Calculate equilibrium concentrations of CO2, H2, CO, and H2O at 800 K, if only CO and H2O are present initially at concentrations of 0.10M each.
solution_text: For the reaction CO (g) + H 2 O (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons {/tex} CO 2 (g) + H 2 (g) (The value of K c = 4.24 at 800K) Initial concentration: 0.1M, 0.1M, 0, 0 Let x mole per litre of each of the products being formed. At equilibrium: (0.1 - x)M, (0.1-x)M, x M, x M where x is the amount of CO 2 and H 2 at equilibrium. The equilibrium constant can be written as, K c = {tex}\frac{[CO_2] [H_2]}{[CO] [H_2O]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^2}{(0.1 - x )^2}{/tex} = 4.24 x 2 = 4.24(0.01 + x 2 - 0.2x) x 2 = 0.0424 + 4.24x 2 - 0.848x 3.24x 2 - 0.848x + 0.0424 = 0 a = 3.24, b = -0.848, c = 0.0424 (for quadratic equation ax 2 + bx + c = 0 x = {tex}\frac{(-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c})}{2 a}{/tex} {tex}x = {-(-0.848) \pm \sqrt{(-0.848)^2-4(3.24)(0.0424)} \over 2(3.24)}{/tex} {tex}x = {-0.848 \pm {0.4118} \over 6.48}{/tex} x 1 = (0.848 – 0.4118)/6.48 = 0.067 x 2 = (0.848 + 0.4118)/6.48 = 0.194 the value 0.194 should be neglected because it will give concentration of the reactant which is more than initial concentration. Therefore, the equilibrium concentrations of CO 2 , H 2 , CO and H 2 O at 800 K are, [CO 2 ] = [H 2- ] = x = 0.067 M [CO] = [H 2 O] = 0.1 – 0.067 = 0.033 M
solution_html:
For the reaction CO (g) + H2O (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons {/tex} CO2(g) + H2(g) (The value of Kc = 4.24 at 800K)
Initial concentration:
0.1M, 0.1M, 0, 0
Let x mole per litre of each of the products being formed.
At equilibrium:
(0.1 - x)M, (0.1-x)M, x M, x M
where x is the amount of CO2 and H2 at equilibrium.
The equilibrium constant can be written as,
Kc= {tex}\frac{[CO_2] [H_2]}{[CO] [H_2O]}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{x^2}{(0.1 - x )^2}{/tex} = 4.24
x2 = 4.24(0.01 + x2 - 0.2x)
x2 = 0.0424 + 4.24x2 - 0.848x
3.24x2 - 0.848x + 0.0424 = 0
a = 3.24, b = -0.848, c = 0.0424
(for quadratic equation ax2 + bx + c = 0
x = {tex}\frac{(-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c})}{2 a}{/tex}
{tex}x = {-(-0.848) \pm \sqrt{(-0.848)^2-4(3.24)(0.0424)} \over 2(3.24)}{/tex}
{tex}x = {-0.848 \pm {0.4118} \over 6.48}{/tex}
x1 = (0.848 – 0.4118)/6.48 = 0.067
x2 = (0.848 + 0.4118)/6.48 = 0.194
the value 0.194 should be neglected because it will give concentration of the reactant which is more than initial concentration.
Therefore, the equilibrium concentrations of CO2, H2, CO and H2O at 800 K are,
[CO2] = [H2-] = x = 0.067 M
[CO] = [H2O] = 0.1 – 0.067 = 0.033 M

question_text: For the equilibrium, 2NOCl(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO(g) + Cl 2 (g) the value of the equilibrium constant, K c is 3.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –6 at 1069 K. Calculate the K p for the reaction at this temperature?
question_html:
For the equilibrium,
2NOCl(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO(g) + Cl2(g)
the value of the equilibrium constant, Kc is 3.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–6 at 1069 K. Calculate the Kp for the reaction at this temperature?
solution_text: We know that, K p = K c (RT) {tex}\Delta{/tex} n For the above reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex} n = (2 + 1) – 2 = 1 K p = 3.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –6 (0.0831 {tex}\times{/tex} 1069) K p = 0.033
solution_html:
We know that,
Kp = Kc (RT){tex}\Delta{/tex}n
For the above reaction,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}n = (2 + 1) – 2 = 1
Kp = 3.75 {tex}\times{/tex}10–6 (0.0831 {tex}\times{/tex} 1069)
Kp = 0.033

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The value of K p for the reaction, CO 2 (g) + C(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2CO (g) is 3.0 at 1000 K. If initially, {tex}P_{CO_{2}}{/tex} = 0.48 bar and P CO = 0 bar and pure graphite is present, calculate the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO 2 .
question_html:
The value of Kp for the reaction,
CO2(g) + C(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2CO (g)
is 3.0 at 1000 K. If initially, {tex}P_{CO_{2}}{/tex} = 0.48 bar and PCO = 0 bar and pure graphite is present, calculate the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO2.
solution_text: For the reaction, let ‘x’ be the decrease in pressure of CO 2 , then CO 2 (g) + C(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2CO(g) Initial pressure: 0.48 bar 0 At equilibrium: (0.48 – x)bar 2x bar {tex}K_{p}=\frac{p_{C O}^{2}}{p_{C O_{2}}}{/tex} K p = (2x) 2 /(0.48 – x) = 3 4x 2 = 3(0.48 – x) 4x 2 = 1.44 – x 4x 2 + 3x – 1.44 = 0 a = 4, b = 3, c = –1.44 x = {tex}\frac{(-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c})}{2 a}{/tex} {tex}x = {-3 \pm \sqrt{3^2-4(4)(-1.44)} \over 2(4)}{/tex} = (–3 {tex}\pm{/tex} 5.66)/8 = (–3 + 5.66)/8 (as value of x cannot be negative hence we neglect that value) x = 2.66/8 = 0.33 The equilibrium partial pressures are, {tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = 2x = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.33 = 0.66 bar {tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = 0.48 – x = 0.48 – 0.33 = 0.15 bar
solution_html:
For the reaction,
let ‘x’ be the decrease in pressure of CO2, then
CO2(g) + C(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2CO(g)
Initial
pressure: 0.48 bar 0
At equilibrium: (0.48 – x)bar 2x bar
{tex}K_{p}=\frac{p_{C O}^{2}}{p_{C O_{2}}}{/tex}
Kp = (2x)2/(0.48 – x) = 3
4x2 = 3(0.48 – x)
4x2 = 1.44 – x
4x2 + 3x – 1.44 = 0
a = 4, b = 3, c = –1.44
x = {tex}\frac{(-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c})}{2 a}{/tex}
{tex}x = {-3 \pm \sqrt{3^2-4(4)(-1.44)} \over 2(4)}{/tex}
= (–3 {tex}\pm{/tex} 5.66)/8
= (–3 + 5.66)/8 (as value of x cannot be negative hence we neglect that value)
x = 2.66/8 = 0.33
The equilibrium partial pressures are,
{tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = 2x = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.33 = 0.66 bar
{tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = 0.48 – x = 0.48 – 0.33 = 0.15 bar

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The value of K c for the reaction 2A {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} B + C is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 . At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is [A] = [B] = [C] = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 M. In which direction the reaction will proceed?
question_html: The value of Kc for the reaction 2A {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} B + C is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3. At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is [A] = [B] = [C] = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M. In which direction the reaction will proceed?
solution_text: For the reaction, the reaction quotient, Q c is {tex}Q_c = \frac { [ B ] [ C ] } { [ A ] ^ { 2 } }{/tex} As [A] = [B] = [C] = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 M {tex}Q_c = \frac { \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \right) \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \right) } { \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = 1{/tex} It is given that K c = 2 x 10 -3. Hence Q c > K c , so the reaction will proceed in the reverse direction i.e. towards reactant A
solution_html:
For the reaction, the reaction quotient, Qc is 
{tex}Q_c = \frac { [ B ] [ C ] } { [ A ] ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
As [A] = [B] = [C] = 3{tex}\times{/tex}10-4 M
{tex}Q_c = \frac { \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \right) \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \right) } { \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = 1{/tex}
It is given that Kc = 2 x 10-3.
Hence Qc > Kc, so the reaction will proceed in the reverse direction i.e. towards reactant A

question_text: 13.8g of N 2 O 4 was placed in a 1L reaction vessel at 400K and allowed to attain equilibrium N 2 O 4 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO 2 (g) The total pressure at equilibrium was found to be 9.15 bar. Calculate K c , K p and partial pressure at equilibrium.
question_html:
13.8g of N2O4 was placed in a 1L reaction vessel at 400K and allowed to attain equilibrium
N2O4 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO2 (g)
The total pressure at equilibrium was found to be 9.15 bar. Calculate Kc, Kp and partial pressure at equilibrium.
solution_text: We know pV = nRT Total volume (V ) = 1 L Molecular mass of N 2 O 4 = 92 g Number of moles = 13.8g/92 g = 0.15 of the gas (n) Gas constant (R) = 0.083 bar L mol –1 K –1 Temperature (T ) = 400 K pV = nRT p {tex}\times{/tex} 1L = 0.15 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 0.083 bar L mol –1 K –1 {tex}\times{/tex} 400 K p = 4.98 bar N 2 O 4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO 2 Initial pressure: 4.98 bar 0 At equilibrium: (4.98 – x) bar 2x bar Hence, P total at equilibrium = {tex}P_{{N_2}{O_4}} + P_{NO_2}{/tex} 9.15 = (4.98 – x) + 2x 9.15 = 4.98 + x x = 9.15 – 4.98 = 4.17 bar Partial pressures at equilibrium are, {tex}P_{{N_2}{O_4}}{/tex} = 4.98 – 4.17 = 0.81bar {tex}P_{NO_2}{/tex} = 2x = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.17 = 8.34 bar Kp = {tex}\left(p_{NO_{2}}\right)^{2} / p_{N_{2}O_{4}}{/tex} = (8.34) 2 /0.81 = 85.87 K p = K c (RT) {tex}\Delta{/tex} n 85.87 = Kc (0.083 {tex}\times{/tex} 400) 1 K c = 2.586 = 2.6
solution_html:
We know pV = nRT
Total volume (V ) = 1 L
Molecular mass of N2O4 = 92 g
Number of moles = 13.8g/92 g = 0.15 of the gas (n)
Gas constant (R) = 0.083 bar L mol–1K–1
Temperature (T ) = 400 K
pV = nRT
p {tex}\times{/tex} 1L = 0.15 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 0.083 bar L mol–1K–1 {tex}\times{/tex} 400 K
p = 4.98 bar
N2O4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO2
Initial pressure: 4.98 bar 0
At equilibrium: (4.98 – x) bar 2x bar
Hence,
Ptotal at equilibrium = {tex}P_{{N_2}{O_4}} + P_{NO_2}{/tex}
9.15 = (4.98 – x) + 2x
9.15 = 4.98 + x
x = 9.15 – 4.98 = 4.17 bar
Partial pressures at equilibrium are,
{tex}P_{{N_2}{O_4}}{/tex} = 4.98 – 4.17 = 0.81bar
{tex}P_{NO_2}{/tex} = 2x = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.17 = 8.34 bar
Kp = {tex}\left(p_{NO_{2}}\right)^{2} / p_{N_{2}O_{4}}{/tex}
= (8.34)2/0.81 = 85.87
Kp = Kc (RT){tex}\Delta{/tex}n
85.87 = Kc (0.083 {tex}\times{/tex} 400)1
Kc = 2.586 = 2.6

question_text: 3.00 mol of PCl 5 kept in 1L closed reaction vessel was allowed to attain equilibrium at 380K. Calculate the composition of the mixture at equilibrium. K c = 1.80
question_html: 3.00 mol of PCl5 kept in 1L closed reaction vessel was allowed to attain equilibrium at 380K. Calculate the composition of the mixture at equilibrium. Kc = 1.80
solution_text: PCl 5 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCl 3 + Cl 2 Initial concentration: 3.0, 0, 0 Let x mol per litre of PCl 5 be dissociated, At equilibrium: (3-x), x, x {tex}K_{c}=\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] /\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]{/tex} 1.8 = x 2 /(3 – x) x 2 + 1.8x – 5.4 = 0 {tex}x = {-1.8 \pm \sqrt{(1.8)^2 -4(-5.4)} \over 2}{/tex} {tex}x = {-1.8 \pm \sqrt{3.24-21.6} \over 2}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{-1.8 + 4.98}{2}{/tex} x = 1.59 [PCl 5 ] = 3.0 – x = 3 –1.59 = 1.41 M [PCl 3 ] = [Cl 2 ] = x = 1.59 M
solution_html:
PCl5 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCl3 + Cl2
Initial
concentration: 3.0, 0, 0
Let x mol per litre of PCl5 be dissociated,
At equilibrium:
(3-x), x, x
{tex}K_{c}=\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] /\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]{/tex}
1.8 = x2/(3 – x)
x2 + 1.8x – 5.4 = 0
{tex}x = {-1.8 \pm \sqrt{(1.8)^2 -4(-5.4)} \over 2}{/tex}
{tex}x = {-1.8 \pm \sqrt{3.24-21.6} \over 2}{/tex}
{tex}x = \frac{-1.8 + 4.98}{2}{/tex}
x = 1.59
[PCl5] = 3.0 – x = 3 –1.59 = 1.41 M
[PCl3] = [Cl2] = x = 1.59 M

question_text: The value of {tex}\Delta G^{\ominus}{/tex} for the phosphorylation of glucose in glycolysis is 13.8 kJ/mol. Find the value of K c at 298 K.
question_html: The value of {tex}\Delta G^{\ominus}{/tex} for the phosphorylation of glucose in glycolysis is 13.8 kJ/mol. Find the value of Kc at 298 K.
solution_text: {tex}\Delta G^{\ominus}{/tex} = 13.8 kJ/mol = 13.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 J/mol Also, {tex}\Delta G^{\ominus}{/tex} = – RT lnK c Hence, ln K c = –13.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 J/mol (8.314 J mol –1 K –1 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 K) ln Kc = – 5.569 K c = e –5.569 K c = 3.81 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta G^{\ominus}{/tex} = 13.8 kJ/mol = 13.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 103J/mol
Also, {tex}\Delta G^{\ominus}{/tex} = – RT lnKc
Hence, ln Kc = –13.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 103J/mol (8.314 J mol –1K–1 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 K)
ln Kc = – 5.569

Kc = 3.81 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3

question_html:
Classify the following species into Lewis acids and Lewis bases and show how these act as Lewis acid/base:

  1. OH-
  2. F-
  3. H+
  4. BCl3
solution_text: Hydroxyl ion (OH - ) acts as a Lewis base as it can donate an electron lone pair. Fluoride ion (F - ) acts as a Lewis base as it can donate any one of its four electron lone pairs. A proton (H + ) acts as a Lewis acid as it can accept a lone pair of electrons from bases like hydroxyl ion and fluoride ion. BCl 3 acts as a Lewis acid as it can accept a lone pair of electrons from species like ammonia or amine molecules.
solution_html:
1. Hydroxyl ion (OH-) acts as a Lewis base as it can donate an electron lone pair.
  2. Fluoride ion (F-) acts as a Lewis base as it can donate any one of its four electron lone pairs.
  3. A proton (H+) acts as a Lewis acid as it can accept a lone pair of electrons from bases like hydroxyl ion and fluoride ion.
  4. BCl3 acts as a Lewis acid as it can accept a lone pair of electrons from species like ammonia or amine molecules.

solution_html: pH = -log[H+] {tex} = - \log (3.8 \times {10^{ - 3}}) = - \log 3.8 + 3 = 3 - 0.5798{/tex} = 2.4202  = 2.42

question_text: Calculate the pH of 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 M solution of HCl.
question_html: Calculate the pH of 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 M solution of HCl.
solution_text: 2H 2 O (l) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H 3 O + (aq) + OH – (aq) K w = [OH – ][H 3 O + ] = 10 -14 Let, x = [OH - ] = [H 3 O + ] from H 2 O. The H 3 O + concentration is generated (i) from the ionization of HCl dissolved i.e., HCl(aq) + H 2 O(l) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H 3 O + (aq) + Cl - (aq), and (ii) from ionization of H 2 O. In these very dilute solutions, both sources of H 3 O + must be considered: [H 3 O + ] = 10 -8 + x K w = (10 -8 + x) (x) = 10 -14 or x 2 + 10 -8 x - 10 -14 = 0 [OH - ] = x = 9.5 × 10 -8 So, pOH = 7.02 and pH = 6.98
solution_html:
2H2O (l) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H3O+ (aq) + OH– (aq)
Kw = [OH–][H3O+] = 10-14
Let, x = [OH-] = [H3O+] from H2O. The H3O+ concentration is generated (i) from the ionization of HCl dissolved i.e., HCl(aq) + H2O(l) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H3O+ (aq) + Cl- (aq), and (ii) from ionization of H2O. In these very dilute solutions, both sources of H3O+ must be considered:
[H3O+] = 10-8 + x
Kw = (10-8 + x) (x) = 10-14
or x2 + 10-8 x - 10-14 = 0
[OH-] = x = 9.5 × 10-8
So, pOH = 7.02 and pH = 6.98

question_text: The ionization constant of HF is 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –4 . Calculate the degree of dissociation of HF in its 0.02 M solution. Calculate the concentration of all species present (H 3 O + , F – and HF) in the solution and its pH.
question_html: The ionization constant of HF is 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4. Calculate the degree of dissociation of HF in its 0.02 M solution. Calculate the concentration of all species present (H3O+, F– and HF) in the solution and its pH.
solution_text: The following proton transfer reactions are possible: HF + H 2 O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H 3 O + + F - K a = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –4 H 2 O + H 2 O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H 3 O + + OH - K w = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 As K a >> K w , [1] is the principle reaction. Substituting equilibrium concentrations in the equilibrium reaction for principal reaction gives: K a = (0.02 {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) 2 / (0.02 – 0.02 {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) = 0.02 {tex}\alpha{/tex} 2 / (1 – {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –4 We obtain the following quadratic equation: {tex}\alpha{/tex} 2 + 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} – 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –2 = 0 The quadratic equation in {tex}\alpha{/tex} can be solved and the two values of the roots are: {tex}\alpha{/tex} = + 0.12 and – 0.12 The negative root is not acceptable and hence, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.12 This means that the degree of ionization, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.12, then equilibrium concentrations of other species viz., HF, F – and H 3 O + are given by: {tex}\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]=[\mathrm{F^-}]=\mathrm{c} \alpha=0.02 \times 0.12{/tex} = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 M {tex}[\mathrm{HF}]=\mathrm{c}(1-\alpha)=0.02(1-0.12){/tex} = 17.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M pH = – log[H + ] = –log(2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 ) = 2.62
solution_html:
The following proton transfer reactions are possible:

  1. HF + H2O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H3O+ + F-  Ka = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4
  2. H2O + H2O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H3O+ + OH- Kw = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–14
    As Ka >> Kw, [1] is the principle reaction.

    Substituting equilibrium concentrations in the equilibrium reaction for principal reaction gives:
    Ka = (0.02{tex}\alpha{/tex})2 / (0.02 – 0.02{tex}\alpha{/tex})
    = 0.02 {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 / (1 –{tex}\alpha{/tex}) = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4
    We obtain the following quadratic equation:
    {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 + 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–2{tex}\alpha{/tex} – 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–2 = 0
    The quadratic equation in {tex}\alpha{/tex} can be solved and the two values of the roots are:
    {tex}\alpha{/tex} = + 0.12 and – 0.12
    The negative root is not acceptable and hence,
    {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.12
    This means that the degree of ionization, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.12, then equilibrium concentrations of other species viz., HF, F– and H3O+ are given by:
    {tex}\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]=[\mathrm{F^-}]=\mathrm{c} \alpha=0.02 \times 0.12{/tex}
    = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3 M
    {tex}[\mathrm{HF}]=\mathrm{c}(1-\alpha)=0.02(1-0.12){/tex}
    = 17.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M
    pH = – log[H+] = –log(2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3) = 2.62

question_text: The pH of 0.1 M monobasic acid is 4.50. Calculate the concentration of species H + , A - and HA at equilibrium. Also, determine the value of K a and pK a of the monobasic acid.
question_html: The pH of 0.1 M monobasic acid is 4.50. Calculate the concentration of species H+, A- and HA at equilibrium. Also, determine the value of Ka and pKa of the monobasic acid.
solution_text: According to the question, pH of 0.1 M monobasic acid is 4.50. We know that, pH = - log [H + ] {tex}\therefore{/tex} [H + ] =10 -pH = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4.50 = 3.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 Also {tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\left[A^{-}\right]=3.16 \times 10^{-5}{/tex} Now, K a = {tex}{ [H^+] [A^-]}\over [HA]{/tex} [HA] = 0.1 - (3.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 ) {tex}\approx 0.1{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} K a = {tex}\frac{\left(3.16 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{2}}{0.1} {/tex} = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 . We know that, pK a = -log(K a ) = -log( 10 -8 ) {tex}\therefore {/tex} pK a = 8.
solution_html:
According to the question,  pH of 0.1 M monobasic acid is 4.50.
We know that, pH = - log [H+]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] =10-pH 
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4.50 
= 3.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
Also {tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\left[A^{-}\right]=3.16 \times 10^{-5}{/tex}
Now, Ka ={tex}{ [H^+] [A^-]}\over [HA]{/tex}
[HA] = 0.1 - (3.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) {tex}\approx 0.1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex}Ka = {tex}\frac{\left(3.16 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{2}}{0.1} {/tex} = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8.
We know that, pKa = -log(Ka)
= -log( 10-8 )
{tex}\therefore {/tex}pKa = 8.

question_text: Calculate the pH of 0.08M solution of hypochlorous acid, HOCl. The ionization constant of the acid is 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 . Determine the percent dissociation of HOCl.
question_html: Calculate the pH of 0.08M solution of hypochlorous acid, HOCl. The ionization constant of the acid is 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5. Determine the percent dissociation of HOCl.
solution_text: Ka = {[H 3 O + ][ClO–] / [HOCl]} = x 2 / (0.08 – x) As x << 0.08, therefore 0.08 – x {tex} \simeq {/tex} 0.08 x 2 / 0.08 = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 x 2 = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –6 , thus, x = 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 [H + ] = 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 M. Therefore, Percent dissociation = {[HOCl] dissociated / [HOCl] initial } {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 / 0.08 = 1.76 %. pH = –log(1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 ) = 2.85.

Ka = {[H3O+][ClO–] / [HOCl]}
= x2 / (0.08 – x)
As x << 0.08, therefore 0.08 – x {tex} \simeq {/tex} 0.08
x2 / 0.08 = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5
x2 = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–6, thus, x = 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3
[H+] = 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3 M.
Therefore,
Percent dissociation
= {[HOCl]dissociated / [HOCl]initial } {tex}\times{/tex} 100
= 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3 {tex}\times{/tex} 102/ 0.08 = 1.76 %.
pH = –log(1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3) = 2.85.

question_text: The pH of 0.004 M hydrazine solution is 9.7. Calculate its ionisation constant K b and pK b .
question_html: The pH of 0.004 M hydrazine solution is 9.7. Calculate its ionisation constant Kb and pKb.
solution_text: Hydrazine (NH 2 NH 2 ) undergoes ionisation in water as follows: NH 2 NH 2 + H 2 O {tex}\rightleftharpoons {/tex} NH 2 NH 3 + + OH - ..(i) Given: pH = 9.7 Thus, - log [H + ] = 9.7 [H + ] = antilog (-9.7) = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 We know that ionic product of water (K w ) = 1 x 10 -14 Thus, [H + ] [OH - ] = 10 -14 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 {tex}\times{/tex} [OH - ] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 [OH - ] = {tex}\frac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{1.67 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 5.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 From equation (i) it is observed that [OH - ] = [NH 2 NH 3 + ] So [NH 2 NH 3 + ] = 5.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 Now, K b = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{3}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(5.98\times10^{-5}\right)\;\times\left(5.98\times10^{-5}\right)}{0.004}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(5.98\times10^{-5}\right)^2}{0.004}{/tex} {tex}=8.96\times10^{-7}{/tex} So pK b = - log K b = - log(8.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 ) = 6.04
solution_html:
Hydrazine (NH2NH2) undergoes ionisation in water as follows:
NH2NH2 + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons {/tex} NH2NH3+ + OH- ..(i)
Given: pH = 9.7
Thus,
- log [H+] = 9.7
[H+] = antilog (-9.7) = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
We know that ionic product of water (Kw) = 1 x 10-14
Thus,
[H+] [OH-] = 10-14
1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 {tex}\times{/tex} [OH-] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14
[OH-] = {tex}\frac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{1.67 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 5.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 
From equation (i) it is observed that [OH-] = [NH2NH3+]
So  [NH2NH3+] = 5.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 
Now,
Kb = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{3}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}\right]}{/tex} 
 {tex}=\frac{\left(5.98\times10^{-5}\right)\;\times\left(5.98\times10^{-5}\right)}{0.004}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\left(5.98\times10^{-5}\right)^2}{0.004}{/tex}
{tex}=8.96\times10^{-7}{/tex}
So pKb = - log Kb = - log(8.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7) = 6.04

question_text: Calculate the pH of the solution in which 0.2M NH 4 Cl and 0.1M NH 3 are present. The pK b of ammonia solution is 4.75.
question_html: Calculate the pH of the solution in which 0.2M NH4Cl and 0.1M NH3 are present. The pKb of ammonia solution is 4.75.
solution_text: NH 3 + H 2 O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} + OH – The ionization constant of NH 3 , K b = antilog (–pK b ) i.e. K b = [ {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ][OH – ] / [NH 3 ] = (0.20 + x)(x)/(0.1 – x) = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 As K b is small, we can neglect x in comparison to 0.1M and 0.2M. Thus, [OH – ] = x = 0.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 Therefore, [H + ] = 1.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –9 pH = – log[H + ] = 8.95.

question_text: Determine the degree of ionization and pH of a 0.05M of ammonia solution. The ionization constant of ammonia can be taken from Table 6.7. Also, calculate the ionization constant of the conjugate acid of ammonia.
question_html: Determine the degree of ionization and pH of a 0.05M of ammonia solution. The ionization constant of ammonia can be taken from Table 6.7. Also, calculate the ionization constant of the conjugate acid of ammonia.
solution_text: The ionization of NH 3 in water is represented by equation: {tex}NH_3 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_4^{+} +OH^-{/tex} We use equation (6.33) to calculate hydroxyl ion concentration, [OH– ] = c {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.05 {tex}\alpha{/tex} K b = {tex}\frac {0.05 2\ \alpha^2}{(1 - \alpha)}{/tex} The value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} is small, therefore the quadratic equation can be simplified by neglecting {tex}\alpha{/tex} in comparison to 1 in the denominator on right hand side of the equation, Thus, K b = c {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} or {tex}\alpha = \sqrt (\frac {1.77 \times 10^{–5}}{0.05}){/tex} = 0.018. [OH - ] = c {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.018 = 9.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 M. [H + ] = {tex}\frac {K_w}{[OH^-]} = \frac {10^{-14}}{(9.4 10^{-4})}{/tex} = 1.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 pH = -log(1.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 ) = 10.97 Now, using the relation for conjugate acid-base pair, K a {tex}\times{/tex} K b = K w using the value of K b of NH 3 from Table 6.7. We can determine the concentration of conjugate acid {tex}NH_4^+{/tex} K a = {tex}\frac {K_w}{K_b} = \frac {10^{-14}}{1.77 \times 10^{-5}}{/tex} = 5.64 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 .
solution_html:
The ionization of NH3 in water is represented by equation:
{tex}NH_3 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_4^{+} +OH^-{/tex}
We use equation (6.33) to calculate hydroxyl ion concentration,
[OH– ] = c {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.05 {tex}\alpha{/tex}
Kb = {tex}\frac {0.05 2\ \alpha^2}{(1 - \alpha)}{/tex}
The value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} is small, therefore the quadratic equation can be simplified by neglecting {tex}\alpha{/tex} in comparison to 1 in the denominator on right hand side of the equation,
Thus, Kb = c {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} or {tex}\alpha = \sqrt (\frac {1.77 \times 10^{–5}}{0.05}){/tex} = 0.018.
[OH-] = c {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.018 = 9.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4M.
[H+] = {tex}\frac {K_w}{[OH^-]} = \frac {10^{-14}}{(9.4  10^{-4})}{/tex} = 1.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11
pH = -log(1.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11) = 10.97
Now, using the relation for conjugate acid-base pair,
Ka {tex}\times{/tex} Kb = Kw
using the value of Kb of NH3 from Table 6.7.
We can determine the concentration of conjugate acid {tex}NH_4^+{/tex}
Ka = {tex}\frac {K_w}{K_b} = \frac {10^{-14}}{1.77 \times 10^{-5}}{/tex} = 5.64 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10.

question_text: Calculate the pH of a 0.10M ammonia solution. Calculate the pH after 50.0 mL of this solution is treated with 25.0 mL of 0.10M HCl. The dissociation constant of ammonia, K b = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5
question_html: Calculate the pH of a 0.10M ammonia solution. Calculate the pH after 50.0 mL of this solution is treated with 25.0 mL of 0.10M HCl. The dissociation constant of ammonia, Kb = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5
solution_text: NH 3 + H 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} + OH - Kb = {tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] /\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]=1.77 \times 10^{-5}{/tex} Before neutralization, {tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]=0.10-\mathrm{x}=0.10{/tex} x 2 / 0.10 = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 Thus, x = 1.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 = [OH – ] Therefore, [H + ] = K w /[OH - ] = 10 -14 / (1.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 ) = 7.51 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –12 pH = –log(7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –12 ) = 11.12 On addition of 25 mL of 0.1M HCl solution (i.e., 2.5 mmol of HCl) to 50 mL of 0.1M ammonia solution (i.e., 5 mmol of NH 3 ), 2.5 mmol of ammonia molecules are neutralized. The resulting 75 mL solution contains the remaining unneutralized 2.5 mmol of NH 3 molecules and 2.5 mmol of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} The resulting 75 mL of solution contains 2.5 mmol of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ions (i.e., 0.033 M) and 2.5 mmol (i.e., 0.033 M ) of neutralised NH 3 molecules. This NH 3 exists in the following equilibrium: The final 75 mL solution after neutralization already contains 2.5 m mol {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ions (i.e. 0.033M), thus total concentration of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ions is given as: [ {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ] = 0.033 + y As y is small, [NH 4 OH] {tex} \simeq {/tex} 0.033 M and [ {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ] {tex} \simeq {/tex} 0.033M. We know, {tex}K_{\mathrm{b}}=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right][\mathrm{OH}] /\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right]{/tex} = y(0.033)/(0.033) = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 M Thus, y = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 = [OH – ] {tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-14} / 1.77 \times 10^{-5}=0.56 \times 10^{-9}{/tex} Hence, pH = 9.24
solution_html:
NH3 + H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} + OH-
Kb = {tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] /\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]=1.77 \times 10^{-5}{/tex}
Before neutralization,
{tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]=0.10-\mathrm{x}=0.10{/tex}
x2 / 0.10 = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5
Thus, x = 1.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3 = [OH–]
Therefore,
[H+] = Kw/[OH-] = 10-14 / (1.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3) = 7.51 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–12
pH = –log(7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–12) = 11.12
On addition of 25 mL of 0.1M HCl solution (i.e., 2.5 mmol of HCl) to 50 mL of 0.1M ammonia solution (i.e., 5 mmol of NH3), 2.5 mmol of ammonia molecules are neutralized. The resulting 75 mL solution contains the remaining unneutralized 2.5 mmol of NH3 molecules and 2.5 mmol of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex}

The resulting 75 mL of solution contains 2.5 mmol of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ions (i.e., 0.033 M) and 2.5 mmol (i.e., 0.033 M ) of neutralised NH3 molecules. This NH3 exists in the following equilibrium:

The final 75 mL solution after neutralization already contains 2.5 m mol {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ions (i.e. 0.033M), thus total concentration of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ions is given as:
[{tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex}] = 0.033 + y
As y is small, [NH4OH] {tex} \simeq {/tex} 0.033 M and [{tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex}] {tex} \simeq {/tex} 0.033M.
We know,
{tex}K_{\mathrm{b}}=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right][\mathrm{OH}] /\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right]{/tex}
= y(0.033)/(0.033) = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5 M
Thus, y = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5 = [OH–]
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-14} / 1.77 \times 10^{-5}=0.56 \times 10^{-9}{/tex}
Hence, pH = 9.24

question_html: The pKa of acetic acid and pKb of ammonium hydroxide are 4.76 and 4.75 respectively. Calculate the pH of ammonium acetate solution.
solution_text: According to the question, the pK a of acetic acid and pK b of ammonium hydroxide are 4.76 and 4.75. We know that, pH = 7 + {tex}\frac 12{/tex} [pK a - pK b ] = 7 + {tex}\frac { 4.76 - 4.75 } { 2 }{/tex} = 7 + {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } [ 0.01 ]{/tex} = 7 + 0.005 = 7.005

question_text: Calculate the solubility of A 2 X 3 in pure water, assuming that neither kind of ion reacts with water. The solubility product of A 2 X 3 , K sp = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –23 .
question_html: Calculate the solubility of A2X3 in pure water, assuming that neither kind of ion reacts with water. The solubility product of A2X3, Ksp = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–23.
solution_text: {tex}\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{A}^{3 +}+3 \mathrm{X}^{2-}{/tex} {tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{A}^{3+}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{X}^{2-}\right]^{3}=1.1 \times 10^{-23}{/tex} If S = solubility of A 2 X 3 , then {tex}\left[\mathrm{A}^{3+}\right]=2 \mathrm{S} ;\left[\mathrm{X}^{2}\right]=3 \mathrm{S}{/tex} therefore, K sp = (2S) 2 (3S) 3 = 108S 5 = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –23 thus, S 5 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –25 S = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 mol/L
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{A}^{3 +}+3 \mathrm{X}^{2-}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{A}^{3+}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{X}^{2-}\right]^{3}=1.1 \times 10^{-23}{/tex}
If S = solubility of A2X3, then
{tex}\left[\mathrm{A}^{3+}\right]=2 \mathrm{S} ;\left[\mathrm{X}^{2}\right]=3 \mathrm{S}{/tex}
therefore, Ksp = (2S)2(3S)3 = 108S5
= 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–23
thus, S5 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–25
S = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5 mol/L

question_text: The values of K sp of two sparingly soluble salts Ni(OH) 2 and AgCN are 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –15 and 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –17 respectively. Which salt is more soluble? Explain.
question_html: The values of Ksp of two sparingly soluble salts Ni(OH)2 and AgCN are 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–15 and 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–17 respectively. Which salt is more soluble? Explain.
solution_text: {tex}\mathrm{AgCN} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{CN^{-}}{/tex} K sp = [Ag + ][CN – ] = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –17 Ni(OH) 2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ni 2+ + 2OH – {tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}=2 \times 10^{-15}{/tex} Let [Ag + ] = S 1 , then [CN - ] = S 1 Let [Ni 2+ ] = S 2 , then [OH – ] = 2S 2 {tex}\mathrm{S}_{1}^{2}=6 \times 10^{-17}, \mathrm{S}_{1}=7.8 \times 10^{-9}{/tex} (S 2 )(2S 2 ) 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –15 , S 2 = 0.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –4 Ni(OH) 2 is more soluble than AgCN.
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{AgCN} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{CN^{-}}{/tex}
Ksp = [Ag+][CN–] = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–17
Ni(OH)2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ni2+ + 2OH–
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}=2 \times 10^{-15}{/tex}
Let [Ag+] = S1, then [CN-] = S1
Let [Ni2+] = S2, then [OH–] = 2S2
{tex}\mathrm{S}_{1}^{2}=6 \times 10^{-17}, \mathrm{S}_{1}=7.8 \times 10^{-9}{/tex}
(S2)(2S2)2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–15, S2 = 0.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4
Ni(OH)2 is more soluble than AgCN.

question_text: Calculate the molar solubility of Ni(OH) 2 in 0.10M NaOH. The ionic product of Ni(OH) 2 is 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 .
question_html: Calculate the molar solubility of Ni(OH)2 in 0.10M NaOH. The ionic product of Ni(OH)2 is 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15.
solution_text: According to the question, ionic product of Ni(OH) 2 is 2.0 × 10 -15 . Reaction: Ni(OH) 2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ni 2+ + 2OH - NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na + + OH - Here, [OH - ] total = (2S + 0.10) So, K sp = [Ni 2+ ] [OH - ] 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 = (S) (0.10 +2S) 2 As K sp is small, 2S << 0.10, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 = S(0.10) 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ni 2+ ] = S = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 M.
solution_html:
According to the question, ionic product of Ni(OH)2 is 2.0 × 10-15.
Reaction:
Ni(OH)2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ni2+ + 2OH-
NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na+ + OH-
Here, [OH-]total = (2S + 0.10) 
So, Ksp = [Ni2+] [OH-]2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 = (S) (0.10 +2S)2
As Ksp is small, 2S << 0.10,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 = S(0.10)2 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ni2+] = S = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 M.

question_text: A liquid is in equilibrium with its vapours in a sealed container at a fixed temperature. The volume of the container is suddenly increased. What is the initial effect of the change on the vapour pressure? How do the rates of evaporation and condensation change initially? What happens when equilibrium is restored finally and what will be the final vapour pressure?
question_html:
A liquid is in equilibrium with its vapours in a sealed container at a fixed temperature. The volume of the container is suddenly increased.

  1. What is the initial effect of the change on the vapour pressure?
  2. How do the rates of evaporation and condensation change initially?
  3. What happens when equilibrium is restored finally and what will be the final vapour pressure?
solution_text: On increasing the volume of the container, the vapour pressure will initially decrease because the same amount of vapours are now distributed over a larger space. On increasing the volume of the container, the rate of evaporation will increase initially because now more space is available. Since the amount of the vapours per unit volume decrease on increasing the volume, therefore, the rate of condensation will decrease initially. Finally, equilibrium will be restored when the rates of the forward and backward processes become equal. However, the vapour pressure will remain unchanged because it depends upon the temperature and not upon the volume of the container. At equilibrium, the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation. The final vapour pressure is equal to the original value.
solution_html:
1. On increasing the volume of the container, the vapour pressure will initially decrease because the same amount of vapours are now distributed over a larger space.
  2. On increasing the volume of the container, the rate of evaporation will increase initially because now more space is available. Since the amount of the vapours per unit volume decrease on increasing the volume, therefore, the rate of condensation will decrease initially.
  3. Finally, equilibrium will be restored when the rates of the forward and backward processes become equal. However, the vapour pressure will remain unchanged because it depends upon the temperature and not upon the volume of the container. At equilibrium, the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation. The final vapour pressure is equal to the original value.

question_text: What is K c for the following equilibrium when the equilibrium concentration of each substance is: [SO 2 ] = 0.6 M, [O 2 ] = 0.82 M and [SO 3 ] = 1.90 M? {tex}2SO_2(g)+O_2(g)\rightleftharpoons2SO_3\;(g)\;{/tex}
question_html:
What is Kc for the following equilibrium when the equilibrium concentration of each substance is:
[SO2] = 0.6 M, [O2] = 0.82 M and [SO3] = 1.90 M?
{tex}2SO_2(g)+O_2(g)\rightleftharpoons2SO_3\;(g)\;{/tex}
solution_text: For the given reaction: {tex}2S{O_2}(g) + {O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2S{O_3}(g){/tex} We have, [SO 2 ] = 0.6 M; [O 2 ] = 0.82 M [SO 3 ] = 1.90 M Therefore, {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{{{[S{O_3}]}^2}}}{{{{[S{O_2}]}^2}[{O_2}]}} = \frac{{(1.9M) \times (1.9M)}}{{(0.6M) \times (0.6M) \times (0.82M)}}{/tex} = 12.229 M -1 = 12.23 L mol -1
solution_html:
For the given reaction: {tex}2S{O_2}(g) + {O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2S{O_3}(g){/tex}
We have, 
[SO2] = 0.6 M;
[O2] = 0.82 M 
[SO3] = 1.90 M
Therefore, {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{{{[S{O_3}]}^2}}}{{{{[S{O_2}]}^2}[{O_2}]}} = \frac{{(1.9M) \times (1.9M)}}{{(0.6M) \times (0.6M) \times (0.82M)}}{/tex}
= 12.229 M-1 = 12.23 L mol -1

question_text: At a certain temperature and total pressure of 10 5 Pa, iodine vapours contain 40% by volume of I atoms {tex}I_2(g)\rightleftharpoons2I\;(g)\;{/tex} . Calculate K p for the equilibrium.
question_html:
At a certain temperature and total pressure of 105 Pa, iodine vapours contain 40% by volume of I atoms
{tex}I_2(g)\rightleftharpoons2I\;(g)\;{/tex}.
Calculate Kp for the equilibrium.
solution_text: Total pressure of equilibrium mixture = 10 5 Pa Partial Pressure of iodine atoms (I) {tex} = \frac{{40}}{{100}} \times ({10^5}Pa) = 0.4 \times {10^5}Pa{/tex} Partial Pressure iodine molecule (I 2 )= {tex}\frac{60}{100}\times10^5\;=\;0.60\;\times\;10^5\;Pa{/tex} According to the equation, the value of equilibrium constant {tex}\mathop {{I_2}(g)}\limits_{(.6 \times {{10}^5}Pa)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {2I(g)}\limits_{(0.4 \times {{10}^5}Pa)} {/tex} {tex}{K_p} = \frac{{p{I^2}}}{{p{I^2}}} = \frac{{{{(0.4 \times {{10}^5}Pa)}^2}}}{{(0.6 \times {{10}^5}Pa)}} = 2.67 \times {10^4}Pa{/tex}
solution_html:
Total pressure of equilibrium mixture = 105 Pa
Partial Pressure of iodine atoms (I) {tex} = \frac{{40}}{{100}} \times ({10^5}Pa) = 0.4 \times {10^5}Pa{/tex}
Partial Pressure iodine molecule (I2)= {tex}\frac{60}{100}\times10^5\;=\;0.60\;\times\;10^5\;Pa{/tex}
According to the equation, the value of equilibrium constant
{tex}\mathop {{I_2}(g)}\limits_{(.6 \times {{10}^5}Pa)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {2I(g)}\limits_{(0.4 \times {{10}^5}Pa)} {/tex}
{tex}{K_p} = \frac{{p{I^2}}}{{p{I^2}}} = \frac{{{{(0.4 \times {{10}^5}Pa)}^2}}}{{(0.6 \times {{10}^5}Pa)}} = 2.67 \times {10^4}Pa{/tex}

question_text: Write the expression for the equilibrium constant, K c for each of the following reactions: 2NOCl (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO (g) + Cl 2 (g) 2Cu(NO 3 ) 2 (s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2CuO (s) + 4NO 2 (g) + O 2 (g) CH 3 COOC 2 H 5 (aq) + H 2 O(l) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CH 3 COOH (aq) + C 2 H 5 OH (aq) Fe 3+ (aq) + 3OH – (aq) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Fe(OH) 3 (s) I 2 (s) + 5F 2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2IF 5
question_html:
Write the expression for the equilibrium constant, Kc for each of the following reactions:

  1. 2NOCl (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO (g) + Cl2 (g)
  2. 2Cu(NO3)2 (s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2CuO (s) + 4NO2 (g) + O2 (g)
  3. CH3COOC2H5(aq) + H2O(l) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CH3COOH (aq) + C2H5OH (aq)
  4. Fe3+ (aq) + 3OH– (aq) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Fe(OH)3 (s)
  5. I2 (s) + 5F2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2IF5
solution_text: The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}(g)]^{2}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(g)\right]}{[\mathrm{NOCl}(g)]^{2}}{/tex} The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{[\mathrm{CuO}(g)]^{2}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)\right]^{4}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(s)\right]^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)\right]^{4}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)\right]{/tex} The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}(a q)\right]}{/tex} The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}(s)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q)\right]^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q)\right]^{3}}{/tex} The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{IF}_{5}(l)\right]^{2}}{\left[1_{2}(s)\right]\left[\mathrm{F}_{2}(g)\right]^{5}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{IF}_{5}(l)\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{F}_{2}(g)\right]^{5}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}(g)]^{2}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(g)\right]}{[\mathrm{NOCl}(g)]^{2}}{/tex}
  2. The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{[\mathrm{CuO}(g)]^{2}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)\right]^{4}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(s)\right]^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)\right]^{4}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)\right]{/tex}
  3. The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}(a q)\right]}{/tex}
  4. The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}(s)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q)\right]^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(a q)\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q)\right]^{3}}{/tex}
  5. The expression for the equilibrium constant is {tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{IF}_{5}(l)\right]^{2}}{\left[1_{2}(s)\right]\left[\mathrm{F}_{2}(g)\right]^{5}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{IF}_{5}(l)\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{F}_{2}(g)\right]^{5}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the value of K c for each of the following equilibria from the value of K p 2NOCl(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g) + Cl 2 (g); K p = {tex}1.8 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} at 500 K CaCO 3 (s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} CaO(s) + CO 2 (g); K p = 167 atm at 1073 K.
question_html:
Find the value of Kc for each of the following equilibria from the value of Kp

  1. 2NOCl(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g) + Cl2(g); Kp = {tex}1.8 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} at 500 K
  2. CaCO3(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} CaO(s) + CO2(g); Kp = 167 atm at 1073 K.
solution_text: K p and K c are related to each other as, K p = K c (RT) {tex}\triangle n_g{/tex} The value of K c can be calculated as follows: {tex}2NOCl(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NO(g) + C{l_2}{/tex} ; K p = {tex}1.8 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} at 500K. {tex}\triangle n_g{/tex} = n p -n r = 3-2 = 1 R = 0.0821 litre atm K -1 mol -1 Kc = {tex}\frac{Kp}{\left(RT\right)^{\triangle n_g}}=\;\frac{1.8\;\times\;10^{-2}}{0.0821\times500}{/tex} = {tex}4.4 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} The value of K c = {tex}4.4 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}CaC{O_3}(s) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g){/tex} K p = 167 atm, {tex}\triangle n_g{/tex} = 1 R = 0.0821 litre atm K -1 mol -1 ; T = 1073 K {tex}Kc=\;\frac{Kp}{\left(RT\right)^{\triangle n_g}}=\;\frac{167}{0.0821\times1073}\;=1.89{/tex} The value of K c = 1.9 mol -1 L
solution_html:
Kp and Kc are related to each other as, Kp = Kc(RT){tex}\triangle n_g{/tex}
The value of Kc can be calculated as follows:

  1. {tex}2NOCl(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NO(g) + C{l_2}{/tex}; Kp = {tex}1.8 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} at 500K.
    {tex}\triangle n_g{/tex} = np -nr = 3-2 = 1
    R = 0.0821 litre atm K-1mol-1
    Kc = {tex}\frac{Kp}{\left(RT\right)^{\triangle n_g}}=\;\frac{1.8\;\times\;10^{-2}}{0.0821\times500}{/tex}
    = {tex}4.4 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
    The value of Kc  = {tex}4.4 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}CaC{O_3}(s) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g){/tex}
    Kp = 167 atm, {tex}\triangle n_g{/tex} = 1
    R = 0.0821 litre atm K-1 mol-1; T = 1073 K
    {tex}Kc=\;\frac{Kp}{\left(RT\right)^{\triangle n_g}}=\;\frac{167}{0.0821\times1073}\;=1.89{/tex}
    The value of Kc = 1.9 mol-1 L

question_text: For the reaction, K c = 6.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 at 1000 K NO(g) + O 3 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NO 2 (g) + O 2 (g), Both the forward and reverse reactions are elementary bimolecular reactions in equilibrium. What is K c , for the reverse reaction?
question_html:
For the reaction, Kc = 6.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 at 1000 K
NO(g) + O3(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NO2(g) + O2(g),
Both the forward and reverse reactions are elementary bimolecular reactions in equilibrium. What is Kc, for the reverse reaction?
solution_text: For the reaction, NO(g) + O 3 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NO 2 (g) + O 2 (g), K c = 6.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Therefore equilibrium constant for reverse reaction (K' c ) i.e. backward reaction is the inverse of an equilibrium constant for forward reaction (K c ). Hence substituting the value in the given expression K' c = {tex}\frac{1}{K_{c}}=\frac{1}{6.3 \times 10^{14}}{/tex} = 1.59 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15
solution_html:
For the reaction,
NO(g) + O3(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NO2(g) + O2(g), Kc = 6.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014
Therefore equilibrium constant for reverse reaction (K'c) i.e. backward reaction is the inverse of an equilibrium constant for forward reaction (Kc). Hence substituting the value in the given expression
K'c = {tex}\frac{1}{K_{c}}=\frac{1}{6.3 \times 10^{14}}{/tex} = 1.59 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain why pure liquids and solids can be ignored while writing the equilibrium constants expression?
question_html: Explain why pure liquids and solids can be ignored while writing the equilibrium constants expression?
solution_text: The pure liquids and solids can be ignored while writing the equilibrium constants expression because molar concentration of a pure solid or liquid is independent of the amount present. Molar concentration {tex}=\;\frac{Number\;of\;moles}{volume}=\frac{\displaystyle\frac{weight}{Molar\;Mass}}{volume}{/tex} {tex}=\;\frac{weight\;}{volume}\times\frac1{Molar\;Mass}=\;\frac{Density\;}{Molar\;Mass}{/tex} Since the density of a pure liquid or solid is fixed at a given temperature and molar mass is also fixed. Therefore molar concentration is constant.
solution_html:
The pure liquids and solids can be ignored while writing the equilibrium constants expression because molar concentration of a pure solid or liquid is independent of the amount present.
Molar concentration {tex}=\;\frac{Number\;of\;moles}{volume}=\frac{\displaystyle\frac{weight}{Molar\;Mass}}{volume}{/tex}{tex}=\;\frac{weight\;}{volume}\times\frac1{Molar\;Mass}=\;\frac{Density\;}{Molar\;Mass}{/tex}
Since the density of a pure liquid or solid is fixed at a given temperature and molar mass is also fixed. Therefore molar concentration is constant.

question_text: Reaction between N 2 and O 2- takes place as follows: {tex}2{N_2}(g) + {O_3}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2{N_2}O(g){/tex} If a mixture of 0.482 mol of N 2 and 0.933 mol of O 2 is placed in a 10 L reaction vessel and allowed to form N 2 O at a temperature for which {tex}{K_c} = 2.0 \times {10^{ - 37}}{/tex} determines the composition of the equilibrium mixture.
question_html:
Reaction between N2 and O2- takes place as follows:
{tex}2{N_2}(g) + {O_3}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2{N_2}O(g){/tex}
If a mixture of 0.482 mol of N2 and 0.933 mol of O2 is placed in a 10 L reaction vessel and allowed to form N2O at a temperature for which {tex}{K_c} = 2.0 \times {10^{ - 37}}{/tex} determines the composition of the equilibrium mixture.
solution_text: Let x moles of N 2 (g) take part in the reaction. According to the equation, x/2 moles of O 2- (g) will react to form r moles of N 2 O(g). {tex}2{N_2}(g) + {O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2{N_2}O(g){/tex} Initial conc. (Mol/L) [N 2 ] = {tex}\frac{{0.482}}{{10}}{/tex} [O 2- ] = {tex}\frac{{0.933}}{{10}}{/tex} At equilibrium point: {tex}\frac{{0.482 - x}}{{10}}\frac{{0.933 - \frac{x}{2}}}{{10}}\frac{x}{{10}}{/tex} The value of the equilibrium constant {tex}(2.0 \times {10^{ - 37}}){/tex} is extremely small. This means that only small amounts of reactants have reacted. Therefore, x is extremely small and can be omitted as far as the reactants are concerned. Applying Law of Chemical Equilibrium {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{{{[{N_2}O(g)]}^2}}}{{{{[{N_2}(g)]}^2}{{[{O_2}(g)]}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}2.0 \times {10^{ - 37}} = \frac{{{{\left( {\frac{x}{{10}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{{0.482}}{{10}}} \right)}^2} \times \left( {\frac{{0.933}}{{10}}} \right)}} = \frac{{0.01{x^2}}}{{2.1676 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{/tex} {tex}{x^2} = 43.352 \times {10^{ - 40}}{/tex} or {tex}x = 6.6 \times {10^{ - 20}}{/tex} As x is extremely small, it can be neglected. Thus, in the equilibrium mixture Molar conc. of N 2 = 0.0482 mol L -1 Molar conc. of O 2 = 0.0933 mol L -1 Molar conc. of N 2 O {tex} = 0.1 \times x = 0.1 \times 6.6 \times {10^{ - 20}}\;mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex} = 6.6 \times {10^{ - 21}}\;mol\;{L^{ - 2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let x moles of N2(g) take part in the reaction. According to the equation, x/2 moles of O2- (g) will react to form r moles of N2O(g). 
{tex}2{N_2}(g) + {O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2{N_2}O(g){/tex}
Initial conc. (Mol/L) [N2] = {tex}\frac{{0.482}}{{10}}{/tex}  [O2-] = {tex}\frac{{0.933}}{{10}}{/tex}  
At equilibrium point: {tex}\frac{{0.482 - x}}{{10}}\frac{{0.933 - \frac{x}{2}}}{{10}}\frac{x}{{10}}{/tex}
The value of the equilibrium constant {tex}(2.0 \times {10^{ - 37}}){/tex} is extremely small. This means that only small amounts of reactants have reacted. Therefore, x is extremely small and can be omitted as far as the reactants are concerned.
Applying Law of Chemical Equilibrium {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{{{[{N_2}O(g)]}^2}}}{{{{[{N_2}(g)]}^2}{{[{O_2}(g)]}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}2.0 \times {10^{ - 37}} = \frac{{{{\left( {\frac{x}{{10}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{{0.482}}{{10}}} \right)}^2} \times \left( {\frac{{0.933}}{{10}}} \right)}} = \frac{{0.01{x^2}}}{{2.1676 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{/tex}
{tex}{x^2} = 43.352 \times {10^{ - 40}}{/tex} or {tex}x = 6.6 \times {10^{ - 20}}{/tex}
As x is extremely small, it can be neglected.
Thus, in the equilibrium mixture
Molar conc. of N2 = 0.0482 mol L-1
Molar conc. of O2 = 0.0933 mol L-1
Molar conc. of N2O {tex} = 0.1 \times x = 0.1 \times 6.6 \times {10^{ - 20}}\;mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex} = 6.6 \times {10^{ - 21}}\;mol\;{L^{ - 2}}{/tex}

question_text: Nitric oxide reacts with Br 2 and gives nitrosyl bromide as per reaction given below: {tex}2NO(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NOBr(g){/tex} When 0.087 mol of NO and 0.0437 mol of Br 2 are mixed in a closed container at constant temperature, 0.0518 mol of NOBr is obtained at equilibrium. Calculate equilibrium amount of NO and Br 2 .
question_html:
Nitric oxide reacts with Br2 and gives nitrosyl bromide as per reaction given below:
{tex}2NO(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NOBr(g){/tex}
When 0.087 mol of NO and 0.0437 mol of Br2 are mixed in a closed container at constant temperature, 0.0518 mol of NOBr is obtained at equilibrium. Calculate equilibrium amount of NO and Br2.
solution_text: The balanced chemical equation for the reaction is: {tex}2NO(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NOBr(g){/tex} According to the equation, 2 moles of NO (g) react with 1 mole of Br 2 (g) to form 2 moles of NOBr (g). The composition of the equilibrium mixture can be calculated as follows: No. of moles of NOBr (g) formed at equilibrium = 0.0518 mol (given) No. of moles of NO (g) taking part in reaction = 0.087 mol No. of moles of NO (g) left at equilibrium = 0.087 - 0.0518 = 0.0352 mol No. of moles of Br 2 (g) taking part in reaction = {tex}1/2 \times 0.0518{/tex} = 0.0259 mol No. moles of Br 2 (g) left at equilibrium = 0.0437 - 0.0259 = 0.0178 mol The initial molar concentration and equilibrium molar concentration of different species may be represented as: {tex}2NO(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NOBr(g){/tex} NO Br 2 NOBr Initial moles 0.087 0.0437 0 Moles at equilibrium point 0.0352 0.0178 0.0518
solution_html:
The balanced chemical equation for the reaction is:
{tex}2NO(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NOBr(g){/tex}
According to the equation, 2 moles of NO (g) react with 1 mole of Br2(g) to form 2 moles of NOBr (g). The composition of the equilibrium mixture can be calculated as follows:
No. of moles of NOBr (g) formed at equilibrium = 0.0518 mol (given)
No. of moles of NO (g) taking part in reaction = 0.087 mol
No. of moles of NO (g) left at equilibrium = 0.087 - 0.0518 = 0.0352 mol
No. of moles of Br2 (g) taking part in reaction = {tex}1/2 \times 0.0518{/tex} = 0.0259 mol
No. moles of Br2(g) left at equilibrium = 0.0437 - 0.0259 = 0.0178 mol
The initial molar concentration and equilibrium molar concentration of different species may be represented as:
{tex}2NO(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NOBr(g){/tex}

  NO Br2 NOBr
Initial moles 0.087 0.0437 0
Moles at equilibrium point 0.0352 0.0178 0.0518

For the given reaction; {tex}2S{O_2}(g) + {O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2S{O_3}(g){/tex}

T = 450 K

question_text: A sample of HI (g) is placed in a flask at a pressure of 0.2 atm. At equilibrium partial pressure of HI (g) is 0.04 atm. What is K p for the given equilibrium? {tex}2HI(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + {I_2}(g){/tex}
question_html:
A sample of HI (g) is placed in a flask at a pressure of 0.2 atm. At equilibrium partial pressure of HI (g) is 0.04 atm. What is Kp for the given equilibrium?
{tex}2HI(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + {I_2}(g){/tex}
solution_text: Here, p(HI)= 0.04 atm, P(H 2 ) = 0.08 atm p(I 2 ) = 0.08 atm. Therefore, {tex}K_p\;=\;\frac{{P_H}_{{}_2}\times\;P_{I_2}}{\left(P_{HI}\right)^2}=\frac{\left(0.08\;atm\right)\times\left(0.08\;atm\right)}{\left(0.04\;atm\right)^2}=4.0{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, p(HI)= 0.04 atm, P(H2 ) = 0.08 atm p(I2) = 0.08 atm.

Therefore, {tex}K_p\;=\;\frac{{P_H}_{{}_2}\times\;P_{I_2}}{\left(P_{HI}\right)^2}=\frac{\left(0.08\;atm\right)\times\left(0.08\;atm\right)}{\left(0.04\;atm\right)^2}=4.0{/tex}

question_text: A mixture of 1.57 mol of N 2 , 1.92 mol of H 2 and 8.13 mol of NH 3 is introduced into a 20 L reaction vessel at 500 K. At this temperature, the equilibrium constant, K c for the reaction N 2 (g) + 3H 2 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH 3 (g) is {tex}1.7 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} . Is this reaction at equilibrium? If not, what is the direction of net reaction?
question_html: A mixture of 1.57 mol of N2, 1.92 mol of H2 and 8.13 mol of NH3 is introduced into a 20 L reaction vessel at 500 K. At this temperature, the equilibrium constant, Kc for the reaction N2(g) + 3H2(g)  {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3(g) is {tex}1.7 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}. Is this reaction at equilibrium? If not, what is the direction of net reaction?
solution_text: Given, [NH 3 ] = 8.13 {tex}\mathrm{~mol} {/tex} /20L {concentration = {tex}\frac{mole}{volume}{/tex} } = 0.4065M [N 2 ] = 157mol/20L = 0.0785M [H 2 ] = 1.92mol/20L = 0.096M Q c = {tex}\frac{(0.4065 \mathrm{M})^2}{(0.096 \mathrm{M})^3(0.0785 \mathrm{M})}{/tex} = 2.379 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 /M 2 Here, you can see that, K c is not equal to Q c , so the reaction is not in equilibrium. because Q c > K c it means that the reaction will proceed in backward direction or in the direction of reactants .
solution_html:
Given,
[NH3] = 8.13{tex}\mathrm{~mol} {/tex}/20L {concentration = {tex}\frac{mole}{volume}{/tex}}
 = 0.4065M
[N2] = 157mol/20L = 0.0785M
[H2] = 1.92mol/20L = 0.096M
Qc = {tex}\frac{(0.4065 \mathrm{M})^2}{(0.096 \mathrm{M})^3(0.0785 \mathrm{M})}{/tex}
= 2.379 {tex}\times{/tex} 103/M2
Here, you can see that, Kc is not equal to Qc, so the reaction is not in equilibrium.
because Qc > Kc it means that the reaction will proceed in backward direction or in the direction of reactants .

question_text: The equilibrium constant expression for a gas reaction is, {tex}K_c=\frac{\left[NH_3\right]^4\;\left[O_2\right]^5}{\left[NO\right]^4\left[H_2O\right]^6}{/tex} Write the balanced chemical equation corresponding to this expression.
question_html:
The equilibrium constant expression for a gas reaction is,
{tex}K_c=\frac{\left[NH_3\right]^4\;\left[O_2\right]^5}{\left[NO\right]^4\left[H_2O\right]^6}{/tex}
Write the balanced chemical equation corresponding to this expression.
solution_text: The expression shows that 4NO and 6H 2 O are reactants and 4NH 3 and 5O 2 are the products. Thus, the balanced chemical equation is {tex}4NO(g) + 6{H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons 4N{H_3}(g) + 5{O_2}(g){/tex}
solution_html:
The expression shows that 4NO and 6H2O are reactants and 4NH3 and 5O2 are the products. Thus, the balanced chemical equation is
{tex}4NO(g) + 6{H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons 4N{H_3}(g) + 5{O_2}(g){/tex}

question_text: One mole of H 2 O and one mole of CO are taken in 10 L vessel and heated to 725 K. At equilibrium 40% of water (by mass) reacts with CO according to the equation, {tex}{H_2}O(g) + CO(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + C{O_2}(g){/tex} Calculate the equilibrium constant for the reaction.
question_html:
One mole of H2O and one mole of CO are taken in 10 L vessel and heated to 725 K. At equilibrium 40% of water (by mass) reacts with CO according to the equation,
{tex}{H_2}O(g) + CO(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + C{O_2}(g){/tex}
Calculate the equilibrium constant for the reaction.
solution_text: Number of moles of water originally present = 1 mol Percentage of water reacted = 40% Number of moles of water reacted {tex} = \frac{{1 \times 40}}{{100}}{/tex} = 0.4 mol Number of moles of water left = (1 - 0.4) = 0.6 mol: {tex}{H_2}O(g) + CO(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + C{O_2}(g){/tex} H 2 O CO H 2 CO 2 Initial moles/litre {tex}\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} 0 0 Mole/litre at the equilibrium point {tex}\frac{{1 - 0.4}}{{10}} = \frac{{0.6}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{1 - 0.4}}{{10}} = \frac{{0.6}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{0.4}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{0.4}}{{10}}{/tex} Applying law of chemical equilibrium. Equilibrium constant for the reaction {tex}({K_c}) = \frac{{[{H_2}(g)][C{O_2}(g)]}}{{[{H_2}O(g)][CO(g)]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{0.4}}{{10}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}} \right) \times \left( {\frac{{0.4}}{{10}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{0.6}}{{10}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}} \right) \times \left( {\frac{{0.6}}{{10}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.16}}{{0.36}} = 0.44{/tex}
solution_html:
Number of moles of water originally present = 1 mol
Percentage of water reacted = 40%
Number of moles of water reacted {tex} = \frac{{1 \times 40}}{{100}}{/tex} = 0.4 mol
Number of moles of water left = (1 - 0.4) = 0.6 mol:
{tex}{H_2}O(g) + CO(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + C{O_2}(g){/tex}

  H2O CO H2 CO2
Initial moles/litre {tex}\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} 0 0
Mole/litre at the equilibrium point {tex}\frac{{1 - 0.4}}{{10}} = \frac{{0.6}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{1 - 0.4}}{{10}} = \frac{{0.6}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{0.4}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{0.4}}{{10}}{/tex}

Applying law of chemical equilibrium.
Equilibrium constant for the reaction {tex}({K_c}) = \frac{{[{H_2}(g)][C{O_2}(g)]}}{{[{H_2}O(g)][CO(g)]}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{0.4}}{{10}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}} \right) \times \left( {\frac{{0.4}}{{10}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{0.6}}{{10}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}} \right) \times \left( {\frac{{0.6}}{{10}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.16}}{{0.36}} = 0.44{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: At 700 K, the equilibrium constant for the reaction: H 2 (g) + I 2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2HI (g) is 54.8. If 0.5 mol L –1 of HI(g) is present at equilibrium at 700 K, what are the concentration of H 2 (g) and I 2 (g) assuming that we initially started with HI(g) and allowed it to reach equilibrium at 700K?
question_html:
At 700 K, the equilibrium constant for the reaction:
H2(g) + I2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2HI (g)
is 54.8. If 0.5 mol L–1 of HI(g) is present at equilibrium at 700 K, what are the concentration of H2(g) and I2(g) assuming that we initially started with HI(g) and allowed it to reach equilibrium at 700K?
solution_text: For the reaction, H 2 (g) + I 2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2HI (g) Equilibrium constant is given to be 54.8. i.e. {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right] \times\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{/tex} Let x be equilibrium concentration of H 2 at equilibrium. Then the equilibrium concentration of I 2 will also be x. Since initially, there was only HI present, an equal amount of H 2 and I 2 will be produced. At equilibrium: {tex}\mathop {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}\limits_ \times \,{\text{ + }}\,\mathop {{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}\limits_ \times \, \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{2Hl(g)}}}\limits_{0.5} {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right] \times\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(0.5)^{2}}{x^{2}}=54.8{/tex} {tex}x=\sqrt{\frac{(0.5)^{2}}{54.8}}{/tex} = 0.0675 Therefore, the concentration of H 2 and I 2 is 0.0675 mol L -1 .
solution_html:
For the reaction,
H2 (g) + I2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2HI (g)
Equilibrium constant is given to be 54.8.
i.e.{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right] \times\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
Let x be equilibrium concentration of H2 at equilibrium. Then the equilibrium concentration of I2 will also be x. Since initially, there was only HI present, an equal amount of H2 and I2 will be produced.
At equilibrium: {tex}\mathop {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}\limits_ \times \,{\text{ + }}\,\mathop {{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}\limits_ \times \, \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{2Hl(g)}}}\limits_{0.5} {/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right] \times\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(0.5)^{2}}{x^{2}}=54.8{/tex}
{tex}x=\sqrt{\frac{(0.5)^{2}}{54.8}}{/tex}
= 0.0675
Therefore, the concentration of H2 and I2 is 0.0675 mol L-1.

question_text: What is the equilibrium concentration of each of the substances in the equilibrium when the initial concentration of ICI was 0.78 M? 2lCl(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} l 2 (g) + Cl 2 (g); K c = 0.14
question_html:
What is the equilibrium concentration of each of the substances in the equilibrium when the
initial concentration of ICI was 0.78 M?
2lCl(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} l2(g) + Cl2(g); Kc = 0.14
solution_text: Suppose at equilibrium, the molar concentration of both I 2 (g) and Cl 2 (g) is 'x'mol L -1 . {tex}2ICl(g) \rightleftharpoons {I_2}(g) + C{l_2}(g){/tex} ICl I 2 Cl 2 Initial molar conc. 0.78 M 0 0 Eqn. molar conc. (0.78 - 2x) M x x {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{[{I_2}(g)][C{l_2}(g)]}}{{[ICl{{(g)}^2}]}} = \frac{{(x) \times (x)}}{{{{(0.78 - 2x)}^2}}}{/tex} ; {tex}\frac{x}{{(0.78 - 2x)}} = {(0.14)^{1/2}} = 0.374{/tex} or x = 0.374 (0.78 - 2x) x = 0.292 - 0.748 x or 1.748 x = 0.292; {tex}x = \frac{{0.292}}{{1.748}} = 0.167{/tex} Therefore, {tex}[ICl] = (0.78 - 2 \times 0.167){/tex} = (0.78 - 0.334) = 0.446 M [I 2 ] = 0.167 M and [Cl 2 ] = 0.167 M
solution_html:
Suppose at equilibrium, the molar concentration of both I2 (g) and Cl2(g) is 'x'mol L-1.
{tex}2ICl(g) \rightleftharpoons {I_2}(g) + C{l_2}(g){/tex}

  ICl I2 Cl2
Initial molar conc. 0.78 M 0 0
Eqn. molar conc. (0.78 - 2x) M x x

{tex}{K_c} = \frac{{[{I_2}(g)][C{l_2}(g)]}}{{[ICl{{(g)}^2}]}} = \frac{{(x) \times (x)}}{{{{(0.78 - 2x)}^2}}}{/tex} ; {tex}\frac{x}{{(0.78 - 2x)}} = {(0.14)^{1/2}} = 0.374{/tex}
 or x = 0.374 (0.78 - 2x)
x = 0.292 - 0.748 x or 1.748 x = 0.292;  {tex}x = \frac{{0.292}}{{1.748}} = 0.167{/tex}
Therefore,
{tex}[ICl] = (0.78 - 2 \times 0.167){/tex} = (0.78 - 0.334) = 0.446 M
[I2] = 0.167 M and [Cl2] = 0.167 M

question_text: K p = 0.04 atm at 898 K for the equilibrium shown below. What is the equilibrium concentration of C 2 H 6 when it is placed in a flask at 4 atm pressure and allowed to come to equilibrium. {tex}{C_2}{H_6}(g) \rightleftharpoons {C_2}{H_4}(g) + {H_2}(g){/tex}
question_html:
Kp = 0.04 atm at 898 K for the equilibrium shown below. What is the equilibrium concentration of C2H6 when it is placed in a flask at 4 atm pressure and allowed to come to equilibrium.
{tex}{C_2}{H_6}(g) \rightleftharpoons {C_2}{H_4}(g) + {H_2}(g){/tex}
solution_text: The equilibrium in the reaction is: C 2 H 6 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C 2 H 4 (g) + H 2 (g) Initial pressure 4atm 0 0 Eqn. pressure (4 - p) atm p atm p atm {tex}{K_p} = \frac{{p{C_2}{H_4} \times p{H_2}}}{{p{C_2}{H_6}}}{/tex} or {tex}0.04 = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{(4 - p)}}{/tex} p 2 = 0.04(4 - p) or p 2 + 0.04p - 0.16 = 0 {tex}p = \frac{{( - 0.04 \pm \sqrt {0.0016 - 4( - 0.16)} }}{2}{/tex} {tex}p = \frac{{( - 0.04) \pm 0.08}}{2} = \frac{{0.76}}{2} = 0.38{/tex} (By taking positive value) Hence, Equilibrium pressure or concentration of C 2 H 6 = (4 - 0.38) = 3.62 atm = 3.62 atm
solution_html:
The equilibrium in the reaction is:

C2H6(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C2H4(g) + H2(g)

Initial pressure 4atm 0 0
Eqn. pressure (4 - p) atm p atm p atm

{tex}{K_p} = \frac{{p{C_2}{H_4} \times p{H_2}}}{{p{C_2}{H_6}}}{/tex} or {tex}0.04 = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{(4 - p)}}{/tex}
p2 = 0.04(4 - p) or p2 + 0.04p - 0.16 = 0
{tex}p = \frac{{( - 0.04 \pm \sqrt {0.0016 - 4( - 0.16)} }}{2}{/tex}
{tex}p = \frac{{( - 0.04) \pm 0.08}}{2} = \frac{{0.76}}{2} = 0.38{/tex} (By taking positive value)
Hence, Equilibrium pressure or concentration of C2H6 = (4 - 0.38) = 3.62 atm = 3.62 atm

question_text: Ethyl acetate is formed by the reaction of ethanol and acetic acid and the equilibrium is represented as: CH 3 COOH(l) + C 5 H 5 OH(l) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH 3 COOC 2 H 5 (l) + H 2 O(l) Write the concentration ratio (reaction quotient), Q c , for this reaction (note: water is not in excess and is not a solvent in this reaction) At 293 K, if one starts with 1.00 mol of acetic acid and 0.18 mol of ethanol, there is 0.171 mol of ethyl acetate in the final equilibrium mixture. Calculate the equilibrium constant. Starting with 0.5 mol of ethanol and 1.0 mol of acetic acid and maintaining it at 293 K, 0.214 mol of ethyl acetate is found after some time. Has equilibrium been reached?
question_html:
Ethyl acetate is formed by the reaction of ethanol and acetic acid and the equilibrium is represented as:
CH3COOH(l) + C5H5OH(l) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3COOC2H5(l) + H2O(l)

  1. Write the concentration ratio (reaction quotient), Qc, for this reaction (note: water is not in excess and is not a solvent in this reaction)
  2. At 293 K, if one starts with 1.00 mol of acetic acid and 0.18 mol of ethanol, there is 0.171 mol of ethyl acetate in the final equilibrium mixture. Calculate the equilibrium constant.
  3. Starting with 0.5 mol of ethanol and 1.0 mol of acetic acid and maintaining it at 293 K, 0.214 mol of ethyl acetate is found after some time. Has equilibrium been reached?
solution_text: The concentration ratio (concentration quotient) Q c for the reaction is: {tex}{Q_c} = \frac{{[C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}(l)][{H_2}O(l)]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH(l)][{C_2}{H_2}OH(l)]}}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{C{H_3}COOH(l) + }&{{C_2}{H_5}OH(l) \rightleftharpoons }&{C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_2}(l) + } \\ {Initial\;molar\;conc.}&{1.0\;mol}&{0.18\;mol}&0 \\ {Molar\;conc.\;at}&{(1 - 0.171)}&{(0.18 - 0.171)}&{0.171\;mol} \\ {equilibrium\;po\operatorname{int} }&{ = 0.829\;mol}&{ = 0.009}&{} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{H_2}O(l)} \\ 0 \\ {0.171\;mol} \\ {} \end{array}{/tex} Applying Law of Chemical equilibrium {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{[C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_3}](l)[{H_2}O(l)]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH](l)[{C_2}{H_3}OH(l)]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{(0.171\;mol) \times (0.171\;mol)}}{{(0.829\;mol)(0.009\;mol)}} = 3.92{/tex} Therefore, the equilibrium constant is 3.92. {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{C{H_3}COOH(l) + }&{{C_2}{H_5}OH(l) \rightleftharpoons }&{C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}(l)} \\ {Initial\;molar\;conc.}&{1.0\;mol}&{0.5\;mol}&{0.214} \\ {Molar\;conc.\;at}&{1.0 - 0.214}&{0.5 - 0.214}&{} \\ {equilibrium}&{ = 0.786}&{ = 0.286\;mol}&{} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { + {H_2}O(l)} \\ {0.214mol} \\ {} \\ {} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}{Q_c} = \frac{{[C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}(l)][{H_2}O(l)]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH(l)][{C_2}{H_2}OH(l)]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{(0.214mol) \times (0.214\;mol)}}{{(0.286\;mol)(0.786\;mol)}} = 0.204{/tex} Since Q c value 0.204 is less than K c , value 3.92 this means that the equilibrium has not been reached. The reactants are still taking part in the reaction to form the products.
solution_html:
1. The concentration ratio (concentration quotient) Qc for the reaction is:
    {tex}{Q_c} = \frac{{[C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}(l)][{H_2}O(l)]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH(l)][{C_2}{H_2}OH(l)]}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{C{H_3}COOH(l) + }&{{C_2}{H_5}OH(l) \rightleftharpoons }&{C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_2}(l) + } \\ {Initial\;molar\;conc.}&{1.0\;mol}&{0.18\;mol}&0 \\ {Molar\;conc.\;at}&{(1 - 0.171)}&{(0.18 - 0.171)}&{0.171\;mol} \\ {equilibrium\;po\operatorname{int} }&{ = 0.829\;mol}&{ = 0.009}&{} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{H_2}O(l)} \\ 0 \\ {0.171\;mol} \\ {} \end{array}{/tex}
    Applying Law of Chemical equilibrium
    {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{[C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_3}](l)[{H_2}O(l)]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH](l)[{C_2}{H_3}OH(l)]}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{(0.171\;mol) \times (0.171\;mol)}}{{(0.829\;mol)(0.009\;mol)}} = 3.92{/tex}
    Therefore, the equilibrium constant is 3.92.
  3. {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{C{H_3}COOH(l) + }&{{C_2}{H_5}OH(l) \rightleftharpoons }&{C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}(l)} \\ {Initial\;molar\;conc.}&{1.0\;mol}&{0.5\;mol}&{0.214} \\ {Molar\;conc.\;at}&{1.0 - 0.214}&{0.5 - 0.214}&{} \\ {equilibrium}&{ = 0.786}&{ = 0.286\;mol}&{} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { + {H_2}O(l)} \\ {0.214mol} \\ {} \\ {} \end{array}{/tex}
    {tex}{Q_c} = \frac{{[C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}(l)][{H_2}O(l)]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH(l)][{C_2}{H_2}OH(l)]}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{(0.214mol) \times (0.214\;mol)}}{{(0.286\;mol)(0.786\;mol)}} = 0.204{/tex}
    Since Qc value 0.204 is less than Kc, value 3.92 this means that the equilibrium has not been reached. The reactants are still taking part in the reaction to form the products.

question_text: A sample of pure PCl 5 was introduced into an evacuated vessel at 473 K. After equilibrium was attained, concentration of PCl 5 was found to be 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 mol L -1 . If value of K c is 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 , what are the concentrations of PCl 3 and Cl 2 at equilibrium? PCl 5 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl 3 (g) + Cl 2 (g)
question_html:
A sample of pure PCl5 was introduced into an evacuated vessel at 473 K. After equilibrium was attained, concentration of PCl5 was found to be 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 mol L-1. If value of Kc is 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3, what are the concentrations of PCl3 and Cl2 at equilibrium?
PCl5 (g)  {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3 (g) + Cl2(g)
solution_text: Let the initial molar concentration of PCl 5 per litre = x mol Molar concentration of PCl 5 at equilibrium = 0.05 mol Moles of PCl 5 decomposed = (x - 0.05) mol Moles of PCl 3 formed = (x - 0.05) mol Moles of Cl 2 formed = (x - 0.05) mol The molar concentration./ litre of reactants and products before the reaction and at the equilibrium point are: Applying Law of chemical equilibrium, {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{[PC{l_3}][C{l_2}]}}{{[PC{l_5}]}}0.0083 = \frac{{(x - 0.05) \times (x - 0.05)}}{{0.05}}{/tex} {tex}{(x - 0.05)^2} = 0.0083 \times 0.05 = 4.15 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} {tex}(x - 0.05) = {(4.15 \times {10^{ - 4}})^{1/2}} = 2.037 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 0.02\;moles{/tex} x = 0.05 + 0.02 = 0.07 mol The molar concentration of PCl 3 at equilibrium. = x - 0.05 = 0.07 - 0.05 = 0.02 mol The molar concentration of Cl 2 at equilibrium. = x - 0.05 = 0.07 - 0.05 = 0.02 mol
solution_html:
Let the initial molar concentration of PCl5 per litre = x mol
Molar concentration of PCl5 at equilibrium = 0.05 mol
Moles of PCl5 decomposed = (x - 0.05) mol
Moles of PCl3 formed = (x - 0.05) mol
Moles of Cl2 formed = (x - 0.05) mol
The molar concentration./ litre of reactants and products before the reaction and at the equilibrium point are:

Applying Law of chemical equilibrium,
{tex}{K_c} = \frac{{[PC{l_3}][C{l_2}]}}{{[PC{l_5}]}}0.0083 = \frac{{(x - 0.05) \times (x - 0.05)}}{{0.05}}{/tex}
{tex}{(x - 0.05)^2} = 0.0083 \times 0.05 = 4.15 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}
{tex}(x - 0.05) = {(4.15 \times {10^{ - 4}})^{1/2}} = 2.037 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 0.02\;moles{/tex}
x = 0.05 + 0.02 = 0.07 mol
The molar concentration of PCl3 at equilibrium. = x - 0.05 = 0.07 - 0.05 = 0.02 mol
The molar concentration of Cl2 at equilibrium. = x - 0.05 = 0.07 - 0.05 = 0.02 mol
