question_text: One of the reactions that take place in producing steel from iron ore is the reduction of iron (II) oxide by carbon monoxide to give iron metal and CO 2 . FeO(s) + CO(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Fe(s) + CO 2 (g); K p = 0.265 atm at 1050 K What is the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO 2 at 1050 K if the initial pressures are: P co = 1.4 atm and {tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = 0.80 atm?
question_html:
One of the reactions that take place in producing steel from iron ore is the reduction of iron (II) oxide by carbon monoxide to give iron metal and CO2.
FeO(s) + CO(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Fe(s) + CO2(g); Kp = 0.265 atm at 1050 K
What is the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO2 at 1050 K if the initial pressures are: Pco = 1.4 atm and {tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = 0.80 atm?
solution_text: {tex}{Q_p} = \frac{{pc{o_2}}}{{pco}} = \frac{{(0.8\;atm)}}{{(1.4\;atm)}} = 0.571{/tex} Since Q p > k p (0.265), this means that the reaction will move in the backward direction to attain the equilibrium. Therefore, the partial pressure of CO 2 will decrease while that of CO will increase so that the equilibrium may be attained again. Let p atm be the decrease in the partial pressure of CO 2 . Therefore, the partial pressure of CO will increase by the same magnitude i.e. p atm. {tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = (0.8 - p) atm; {tex}P_{CO}{/tex} (g) = (1.4 + p) atm At equilibrium {tex}{K_p} = \frac{{p_{co_2}}}{{p_{co}}} = \frac{{(0.8 - p)\;atm}}{{(1.4 + p)\;atm}} = \frac{{(0.8 - p)}}{{(1.4 + p)}}{/tex} or {tex}0.265 = \frac{{(0.8 - p)}}{{(1.4 + p)}}{/tex} 0.371 + 0.265 p = 0.8 - p or 1.265 p = 0.8 - 0.371 = 0.429 p = 0.429 / 1.265 = 0.339 atm The equilibrium partial pressure of CO is ( {tex}P_{CO}{/tex} ) eq = (1.4 + 0.339) = 1.739 atm The equilibrium partial pressure of CO 2 is ( {tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} )eq = (0.8 - 0.339) = 0.461 atm
solution_html:
{tex}{Q_p} = \frac{{pc{o_2}}}{{pco}} = \frac{{(0.8\;atm)}}{{(1.4\;atm)}} = 0.571{/tex}
Since Qp > kp (0.265), this means that the reaction will move in the backward direction to attain the equilibrium. Therefore, the partial pressure of CO2 will decrease while that of CO will increase so that the equilibrium may be attained again. Let p atm be the decrease in the partial pressure of CO2. Therefore, the partial pressure of CO will increase by the same magnitude i.e. p atm.
{tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = (0.8 - p) atm; {tex}P_{CO}{/tex}(g) = (1.4 + p) atm
At equilibrium {tex}{K_p} = \frac{{p_{co_2}}}{{p_{co}}} = \frac{{(0.8 - p)\;atm}}{{(1.4 + p)\;atm}} = \frac{{(0.8 - p)}}{{(1.4 + p)}}{/tex}
or {tex}0.265 = \frac{{(0.8 - p)}}{{(1.4 + p)}}{/tex}
0.371 + 0.265 p = 0.8 - p or 1.265 p = 0.8 - 0.371 = 0.429
p = 0.429 / 1.265 = 0.339 atm
The equilibrium partial pressure of CO is ({tex}P_{CO}{/tex})eq = (1.4 + 0.339) = 1.739 atm
The equilibrium partial pressure of CO2 is ({tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex})eq = (0.8 - 0.339) = 0.461 atm

question_text: Equilibrium constant, K c for the reaction, N 2 (g) + 3H 2 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH 3 at 500 K is 0.061. At a particular time, the analysis shows that the composition of the reaction mixture is 3.0 mol L -1 of N 2 , 2.0 mol L -1 of H 2 and 0.50 mol L -1 of NH 3 . Is the reaction at equilibrium? If not, in which direction does the reaction tend to proceed to reach the equilibrium?
question_html:
Equilibrium constant, Kc for the reaction, N2 (g) + 3H2 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex}  2NH3 at 500 K is 0.061.
At a particular time, the analysis shows that the composition of the reaction mixture is 3.0 mol L-1 of N2, 2.0 mol L-1 of H2 and 0.50 mol L-1 of NH3. Is the reaction at equilibrium? If not, in which direction does the reaction tend to proceed to reach the equilibrium?
solution_text: The given reaction is: {tex}{N_2}(g) + 3{H_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2N{H_2}(g){/tex} According to available data. [N 2 ] = [3.0]; [H 2 ] = [2.0] and [NH 3 ] = [0.50] {tex}{Q_c} = \frac{{{{[N{H_3}(g)]}^2}}}{{[{N_2}(g)]{{[{H_2}(g)]}^3}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{{[0.50]}^2}}}{{[3.0]{{[2.0]}^3}}} = \frac{{0.25}}{{24}} = 0.0104{/tex} The value of the reaction quotient is smaller than the value of the equilibrium constant. Since the value of Q c is less than that of K c (0.061), the reaction is not in a state of equilibrium. It will proceed in the forward direction till Q c becomes the same as K c .
solution_html:
The given reaction is:
{tex}{N_2}(g) + 3{H_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2N{H_2}(g){/tex}
According to available data.
[N2] = [3.0]; [H2 ] = [2.0] and [NH3] = [0.50]
{tex}{Q_c} = \frac{{{{[N{H_3}(g)]}^2}}}{{[{N_2}(g)]{{[{H_2}(g)]}^3}}}{/tex}{tex} = \frac{{{{[0.50]}^2}}}{{[3.0]{{[2.0]}^3}}} = \frac{{0.25}}{{24}} = 0.0104{/tex}
The value of the reaction quotient is smaller than the value of the equilibrium constant. 
Since the value of Qc is less than that of Kc (0.061), the reaction is not in a state of equilibrium. It will proceed in the forward direction till Qc becomes the same as Kc.

question_text: Bromine monochloride (BrCl) decomposes into bromine and chlorine and reaches the equilibrium: {tex}2BrCl(g) \rightleftharpoons B{r_2}(g) + C{l_2}(g){/tex} for which K c is 32 at 500 K. If initially pure BrCl is present at a concentration of {tex}3.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} , what is its molar concentration in the mixture at equilibrium?
question_html:
Bromine monochloride (BrCl) decomposes into bromine and chlorine and reaches the equilibrium:
{tex}2BrCl(g) \rightleftharpoons B{r_2}(g) + C{l_2}(g){/tex}
for which Kc is 32 at 500 K. If initially pure BrCl is present at a concentration of {tex}3.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}, what is its molar concentration in the mixture at equilibrium?
solution_text: Let x moles of BrCl decompose in order to attain the equilibrium. The initial molar concentration and the molar concentration at equilibrium point of different species may be represented as follows: {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {Initial\;molar/litre} \\ {Moles/litres\;at\;eqn.\;po\operatorname{int} } \end{array}\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2BrCl\;(g)}&{ \rightleftharpoons B{r_2}(g)}&{ + C{l_2}(g)} \\ {0.0033}&0&0 \\ {\;0.0033 - x}&{x/2}&{x/2} \end{array}{/tex} Applying law of chemical equilibrium, {tex}K_c=\;\frac{\left[Br_2\right]\left[Cl_2\right]}{\left[BrCl\right]^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow32=\frac{\left[{\displaystyle\frac x2}\right]\left[{\displaystyle\frac x2}\right]}{\left[0.0033-x\right]^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow5.656\;=\frac{\left[\frac x2\right]}{\left[0.0033-x\right]}{/tex} taking sqaure root on both sides {tex}\frac{x}{{(0.0033 - x)}}{/tex} = 11.31 or 12.31x = 0.037 {tex}x = \frac{{0.037}}{{12.31}} = 0.003{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Molar concentration of BrCl at equilibrium point = 0.0033 - 0.003 = 0.0003 mol L -1 {tex} = 3 \times {10^{ - 4}}\;mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let x moles of BrCl decompose in order to attain the equilibrium. The initial molar concentration and the molar concentration at equilibrium point of different species may be represented as follows:
{tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {Initial\;molar/litre} \\ {Moles/litres\;at\;eqn.\;po\operatorname{int} } \end{array}\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2BrCl\;(g)}&{ \rightleftharpoons B{r_2}(g)}&{ + C{l_2}(g)} \\ {0.0033}&0&0 \\ {\;0.0033 - x}&{x/2}&{x/2} \end{array}{/tex}
Applying law of chemical equilibrium,
{tex}K_c=\;\frac{\left[Br_2\right]\left[Cl_2\right]}{\left[BrCl\right]^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow32=\frac{\left[{\displaystyle\frac x2}\right]\left[{\displaystyle\frac x2}\right]}{\left[0.0033-x\right]^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow5.656\;=\frac{\left[\frac x2\right]}{\left[0.0033-x\right]}{/tex} taking sqaure root on both sides
{tex}\frac{x}{{(0.0033 - x)}}{/tex} = 11.31

or 12.31x = 0.037
{tex}x = \frac{{0.037}}{{12.31}} = 0.003{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Molar concentration of BrCl at equilibrium point = 0.0033 - 0.003
= 0.0003 mol L-1 {tex} = 3 \times {10^{ - 4}}\;mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}

question_text: At 1127 K and 1 atmosphere pressure, a gaseous mixture of CO and CO 2 in equilibrium with solid carbon has 90.55% CO by mass. {tex}C(s) + C{O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2CO(g){/tex} Calculate K c for the reaction at the above temperature.
question_html:
At 1127 K and 1 atmosphere pressure, a gaseous mixture of CO and CO2 in equilibrium with solid carbon has 90.55% CO by mass.
{tex}C(s) + C{O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2CO(g){/tex}
Calculate Kc for the reaction at the above temperature.
solution_text: Let the total mass of the gaseous mixture be 100g. Mass of CO = 90.55g And, mass of CO2 = (100 - 90.55) = 9.45g Now, number of moles of CO, {tex}n_{\mathrm{CO}}=\frac{90.55}{28}{/tex} = 3.234 mol Number of moles of CO2, {tex}n_{\mathrm{CO}_2}=\frac{9.45}{44}{/tex} = 0.215 mol Partial pressure of CO, P CO = {tex}\frac{n_{\mathrm{CO}}}{n_{\mathrm{CO}}+n_{\mathrm{CO}_2}} \times{/tex} P total = {tex}\frac {3.234}{3.234 + 0.215} \times 1{/tex} = 0.938 atm Partial pressure of CO2, {tex}p_{\mathrm{CO}_2}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{CO}_2}}{n_{\mathrm{co}}+n_{\mathrm{CO}_2}} \times p_{\text {total }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.215}{3.234+0.215} \times 1{/tex} = 0.062 atm Therefore, Kp = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{CO}]^2}{\left[\mathrm{CO}_2\right.]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(0.938)^2}{0.062}{/tex} = 14.19 For the given reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex} n = 2 - 1 = 1 We know that K p = K c (RT) {tex}\Delta{/tex} n {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14.19 = K c (0.082 {tex}\times{/tex} 1127) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K c = {tex}\frac {14.19}{0.082 \times1127}{/tex} = 0.154(approximately)
solution_html:
Let the total mass of the gaseous mixture be 100g.
Mass of CO = 90.55g
And, mass of CO2 = (100 - 90.55) = 9.45g
Now, number of moles of CO,
{tex}n_{\mathrm{CO}}=\frac{90.55}{28}{/tex} = 3.234 mol
Number of moles of CO2,
{tex}n_{\mathrm{CO}_2}=\frac{9.45}{44}{/tex} = 0.215 mol
Partial pressure of CO,
PCO = {tex}\frac{n_{\mathrm{CO}}}{n_{\mathrm{CO}}+n_{\mathrm{CO}_2}} \times{/tex} Ptotal
= {tex}\frac {3.234}{3.234 + 0.215} \times 1{/tex}
= 0.938 atm
Partial pressure of CO2,
{tex}p_{\mathrm{CO}_2}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{CO}_2}}{n_{\mathrm{co}}+n_{\mathrm{CO}_2}} \times p_{\text {total }}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.215}{3.234+0.215} \times 1{/tex}
= 0.062 atm
Therefore, Kp = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{CO}]^2}{\left[\mathrm{CO}_2\right.]}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(0.938)^2}{0.062}{/tex}
= 14.19
For the given reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 2 - 1 = 1
We know that
Kp = Kc(RT){tex}\Delta{/tex}n
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14.19 = Kc(0.082 {tex}\times{/tex} 1127)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Kc = {tex}\frac {14.19}{0.082 \times1127}{/tex}
= 0.154(approximately)

question_text: Calculate (a) {tex}\Delta {G^\Theta }{/tex} and (b) the equilibrium constant for the formation of NO 2 from NO and O 2 at 298 K {tex}NO(g) + 1/2\;{O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons N{O_2}(g){/tex} where {tex}{\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }(N{O_2}) = 52.0kJ/mol{/tex} {tex}{\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }(NO) = 87.0kJ/mol{/tex} {tex}{\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }({O_2}) = 0 kJ/mol{/tex}
question_html:
Calculate (a) {tex}\Delta {G^\Theta }{/tex} and (b) the equilibrium constant for the formation of NO2 from NO and O2 at 298 K
{tex}NO(g) + 1/2\;{O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons N{O_2}(g){/tex}
where
{tex}{\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }(N{O_2}) = 52.0kJ/mol{/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }(NO) = 87.0kJ/mol{/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }({O_2}) = 0 kJ/mol{/tex}
solution_text: Step I. Calculation of {tex}\Delta {G^\Theta }{/tex} For the given reaction, Step I. Calculation of ΔG Θ ∆G° = ∆G°( Products) – ∆G°( Reactants) {tex}\Delta {G^\Theta } = {\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }(N{O_2}) - [{\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }(1/2{O_2})]{/tex} = 52.0 - (87 + 0) = -35 kJ mol -1 Step II. Calculation of K c {tex}\Delta {G^\Theta } = - 2.303\;RT\;\log {K_c}{/tex} {tex}\log {K_c} = - \frac{{\Delta {G^\Theta }}}{{2.303RT}} = - \frac{{{{( - 35 \times {{10}^3}J\;mo{l^{ - 1}})}^3}}}{{2.303 \times (8.314kJ\;mo{l^{ - 1}}) \times (298K)}} = 6.134{/tex} K c = antilog 6.314 {tex} = 1.36 \times {10^6}{/tex}
solution_html:
Step I. Calculation of {tex}\Delta {G^\Theta }{/tex}
For the given reaction, 
Step I. Calculation of ΔGΘ
∆G° = ∆G°( Products) – ∆G°( Reactants)
{tex}\Delta {G^\Theta } = {\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }(N{O_2}) - [{\Delta _f}{G^\Theta }(1/2{O_2})]{/tex}
= 52.0 - (87 + 0) = -35 kJ mol-1
Step II. Calculation of Kc
{tex}\Delta {G^\Theta } = - 2.303\;RT\;\log {K_c}{/tex}
{tex}\log {K_c} = - \frac{{\Delta {G^\Theta }}}{{2.303RT}} = - \frac{{{{( - 35 \times {{10}^3}J\;mo{l^{ - 1}})}^3}}}{{2.303 \times (8.314kJ\;mo{l^{ - 1}}) \times (298K)}} = 6.134{/tex}
Kc = antilog 6.314 {tex} = 1.36 \times {10^6}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Does the number of moles of reaction products increase, decrease, or remain the same when each of the following equilibria is subjected to a decrease in pressure by increasing the volume? {tex}PCl_5(g)\;\rightleftharpoons PCl_3(g)+Cl_2\;(g){/tex} {tex}CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons CaC{O_3}(g){/tex} {tex}3Fe(s) + 4{H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons F{e_3}{O_4}(s) + 4{H_2}(g){/tex}
question_html:
Does the number of moles of reaction products increase, decrease, or remain the same when each of the following equilibria is subjected to a decrease in pressure by increasing the volume?

  1. {tex}PCl_5(g)\;\rightleftharpoons PCl_3(g)+Cl_2\;(g){/tex}
  2. {tex}CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons CaC{O_3}(g){/tex}
  3. {tex}3Fe(s) + 4{H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons F{e_3}{O_4}(s) + 4{H_2}(g){/tex}
solution_text: According to Le-Chatelier's principle, on decreasing pressure equilibrium shifts in that direction where pressure increases (i.e, the number of moles in the gaseous state are more). Therefore, Pressure will increase in the forward reaction and the number of moles of products will increase in the given reaction {tex}PCl_5(g)\;\rightleftharpoons PCl_3(g)+Cl_2\;(g){/tex} Pressure will increase in backward reaction and the number of moles of products will decrease in the given reaction {tex}CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons CaC{O_3}(g){/tex} The change in pressure will have no effect on the equilibrium constant and there will be no change in the number of moles, {tex}\triangle n_g=0{/tex} . In the given reaction {tex}3Fe(s) + 4{H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons F{e_3}{O_4}(s) + 4{H_2}(g){/tex}
solution_html:
According to Le-Chatelier's principle, on decreasing pressure equilibrium shifts in that direction where pressure increases (i.e, the number of moles in the gaseous state are more). Therefore,

  1. Pressure will increase in the forward reaction and the number of moles of products will increase in the given reaction
    {tex}PCl_5(g)\;\rightleftharpoons PCl_3(g)+Cl_2\;(g){/tex}
  2. Pressure will increase in backward reaction and the number of moles of products will decrease in the given reaction 
    {tex}CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons CaC{O_3}(g){/tex}
  3. The change in pressure will have no effect on the equilibrium constant and there will be no change in the number of moles, {tex}\triangle n_g=0{/tex}. In the given reaction  
    {tex}3Fe(s) + 4{H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons F{e_3}{O_4}(s) + 4{H_2}(g){/tex}

{tex}{H_2} + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2HBr(g){/tex}
Find the equilibrium pressure of all gases, if 10.0 bar of HBr is introduced into a sealed container at 1024 K.
solution_text: Step I. Calculation of K p . {tex}{H_2}(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2HBr(g){/tex} {tex}{K_p} = {K_c}{(RT)^{\Delta n}} = {K_c}(RT)^\circ {/tex} {tex}(\because 2 - 2 = zero){/tex} {tex}{K_p} = {K_c} = 1.6 \times {10^5}{/tex} Step II. Calculation of partial pressure of gases {tex}{H_2}(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2HBr(g){/tex} H 2 Br 2 HBr Initial pressure 10 bar zero zero Eqm. pressure (10 - P) bar P/2 bar P/2 bar {tex}K_p^t = \frac{{p{H_2} \times pB{r_2}}}{{{p^2}HBr}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{{1.6 \times {{10}^5}}} = \frac{{P/2 \times P/2}}{{{{(10 - P)}^2}}} = \frac{{{P^2}}}{{4{{(10 - P)}^2}}}{/tex} On taking square root {tex}\frac{P}{{4{{(10 - P)}^2}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{{1.6 \times {{10}^5}}}} \right)^{1/2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{2(10 - P)}}{P} = {(1.6 \times {10^5})^{1/2}}{/tex} {tex} = 4 \times {10^2}{/tex} {tex}20 - 2P = 4 \times {10^2}P{/tex} or {tex}P(4 \times {10^2} + 2) = 20{/tex} {tex}P = \frac{{20}}{{(400 + 2)}} = \frac{{20}}{{402}} = 0.050\;bar{/tex} p H2 = 0.025 bar p Br2 = 0.025 bar p HBr = 10 - 0.05 = 9.95 bar = 10.0 bar

Step I. Calculation of Kp.
{tex}{H_2}(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2HBr(g){/tex}
{tex}{K_p} = {K_c}{(RT)^{\Delta n}} = {K_c}(RT)^\circ {/tex}{tex}(\because 2 - 2 = zero){/tex}
{tex}{K_p} = {K_c} = 1.6 \times {10^5}{/tex}
Step II. Calculation of partial pressure of gases

{tex}{H_2}(g) + B{r_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2HBr(g){/tex}

  H2 Br2 HBr
Initial pressure 10 bar zero zero
Eqm. pressure (10 - P) bar P/2 bar P/2 bar

{tex}K_p^t = \frac{{p{H_2} \times pB{r_2}}}{{{p^2}HBr}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{{1.6 \times {{10}^5}}} = \frac{{P/2 \times P/2}}{{{{(10 - P)}^2}}} = \frac{{{P^2}}}{{4{{(10 - P)}^2}}}{/tex}
On taking square root {tex}\frac{P}{{4{{(10 - P)}^2}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{{1.6 \times {{10}^5}}}} \right)^{1/2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{2(10 - P)}}{P} = {(1.6 \times {10^5})^{1/2}}{/tex}
{tex} = 4 \times {10^2}{/tex}
{tex}20 - 2P = 4 \times {10^2}P{/tex} or {tex}P(4 \times {10^2} + 2) = 20{/tex}
{tex}P = \frac{{20}}{{(400 + 2)}} = \frac{{20}}{{402}} = 0.050\;bar{/tex}
pH2 = 0.025 bar pBr2 = 0.025 bar pHBr = 10 - 0.05 = 9.95 bar = 10.0 bar

question_text: Dihydrogen gas is obtained from the natural gas by partial oxidation with steam as per following endothermic reaction: {tex}C{H_4}(g) + {H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons CO(g) + 3{H_2}(g){/tex} Write the expression for K p for the above reaction. How will the value of K p and composition of equilibrium mixture be affected by: increasing the pressure. increasing the temperature, using a catalyst?
question_html:
Dihydrogen gas is obtained from the natural gas by partial oxidation with steam as per following endothermic reaction:
{tex}C{H_4}(g) + {H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons CO(g) + 3{H_2}(g){/tex}

  1. Write the expression for Kp for the above reaction.
  2. How will the value of Kp and composition of equilibrium mixture be affected by:
    1. increasing the pressure.
    2. increasing the temperature,
    3. using a catalyst?
solution_text: CH 4 (g)+H 2 O(g)⇌CO(g)+3H 2 (g) The expression for K p for the reaction is: {tex}K_p\;=\frac{\left(P_{CO}\right)\;.\;\left({P_H}_2\right)^3}{\left(P_{CH_4}\right).\;\left(P_{H_2O}\right)}{/tex} i. As the pressure increases, the equilibrium will shift to the left so that the fewer number of moles are produced. ii. If the temperature is increased, according to Le chatelier's principle, the forward reaction will be favoured as it is endothermic. Therefore, the equilibrium gets shifted to the right and the value of K p will increase. iii. The addition of catalyst will not change the equilibrium since it influences both the forward and the backward reactions to the same extent. But it will be attained more quickly.
solution_html:
CH4(g)+H2O(g)⇌CO(g)+3H2(g)

  1. The expression for Kp for the reaction is:{tex}K_p\;=\frac{\left(P_{CO}\right)\;.\;\left({P_H}_2\right)^3}{\left(P_{CH_4}\right).\;\left(P_{H_2O}\right)}{/tex}
  2. i. As the pressure increases, the equilibrium will shift to the left so that the fewer number of moles are produced.
    ii. If the temperature is increased, according to Le chatelier's principle, the forward reaction will be favoured as it is endothermic. Therefore, the equilibrium gets shifted to the right and the value of Kp will increase.
    iii. The addition of catalyst will not change the equilibrium since it influences both the forward and the backward reactions to the same extent. But it will be attained more quickly.

question_text: Describe the effect of: addition of H 2 addition of CH 3 OH removal of CO removal of CH 3 OH On the equilibrium of the reaction: 2H 2 (g) + CO(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH 3 OH(g)
question_html:
Describe the effect of:

  1. addition of H2
  2. addition of CH3OH
  3. removal of CO
  4. removal of CH3OH

On the equilibrium of the reaction: 2H2(g) + CO(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3OH(g)
solution_text: {tex}2{H_2}(g) + CO(g) \rightleftharpoons C{H_3}OH{/tex} According to Le-Chatelier's principle: When any system at equilibrium for a long period of time is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, volume, or pressure, the system changes to a new equilibrium. This change partly counteracts the applied change. On addition of H 2 (increase in the concentration of reactants), equilibrium will be shifted in the forward direction (more products are formed). On addition of CH 3 OH (increase in concentration or product), equilibrium will be shifted in the backward direction. On removal of CO , equilibrium will be shifted in the backward direction. On removal of CH 3 OH, Equilibrium will be shifted in the forward direction.
solution_html:
{tex}2{H_2}(g) + CO(g) \rightleftharpoons C{H_3}OH{/tex}

According to Le-Chatelier's principle: 
When any system at equilibrium for a long period of time is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, volume, or pressure, the system changes to a new equilibrium. This change partly counteracts the applied change.

  1. On addition of H2 (increase in the concentration of reactants), equilibrium will be shifted in the forward direction (more products are formed).
  2. On addition of CH3OH (increase in concentration or product), equilibrium will be shifted in the backward direction.
  3. On removal of CO , equilibrium will be shifted in the backward direction.
  4. On removal of CH3OH, Equilibrium will be shifted in the forward direction.

question_text: At 473 K, the equilibrium constant K c for the decomposition of phosphorus pentachloride, (PCl 5 ) is {tex}8.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex} . If decomposition proceeds as: {tex}PC{l_5}(g) \rightleftharpoons PC{l_3}(g) + C{l_2}(g);{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = 124.0 KJ mol -1 Write an expression for K c for the reaction What is the value of K c for the reverse reaction at the same temperature? What would be the effect on K c if more of PCl 5 is added pressure is incrased the temperature is increased?
question_html:
At 473 K, the equilibrium constant Kc for the decomposition of phosphorus pentachloride, (PCl5) is {tex}8.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex}. If decomposition proceeds as:
{tex}PC{l_5}(g) \rightleftharpoons PC{l_3}(g) + C{l_2}(g);{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = 124.0 KJ mol-1

  1. Write an expression for Kc for the reaction
  2. What is the value of Kc for the reverse reaction at the same temperature?
  3. What would be the effect on Kc if
    1. more of PCl5 is added
    2. pressure is incrased
    3. the temperature is increased?
solution_text: {tex}PC{l_5}(g) \rightleftharpoons PC{l_3}(g) + C{l_2}(g);{/tex} K c = {tex}8.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex} The equilibrium constant expression for {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{[PC{l_3}(g)][C{l_2}(g)]}}{{[PC{l_5}(g)]}}{/tex} The equilibrium constant expression for the reverse reaction {tex}K_c'\;=\frac1{K_c}=\;\frac1{8.3\;\times10^{-3}}\;=\;120.48\;{/tex} By adding more of PCl 5 , value of K c will remain constant because there is no change in temperature. When pressure is increased, the value of the equilibrium constant remains unaffected. By increasing the temperature, the forward reaction will be favoured since it is endothermic in nature. Therefore, the value of equilibrium constant will increase. The reaction is endothermic. When the temperature is increased, K f ​ is increased. Hence, K c = K f ​/K f ​​ is also increased.
solution_html:
{tex}PC{l_5}(g) \rightleftharpoons PC{l_3}(g) + C{l_2}(g);{/tex} Kc ={tex}8.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex}

  1. The equilibrium constant expression for {tex}{K_c} = \frac{{[PC{l_3}(g)][C{l_2}(g)]}}{{[PC{l_5}(g)]}}{/tex}
  2. The equilibrium constant expression for the reverse reaction {tex}K_c'\;=\frac1{K_c}=\;\frac1{8.3\;\times10^{-3}}\;=\;120.48\;{/tex}
  3.  
    1. By adding more of PCl5, value of Kc will remain constant because there is no change in temperature.
    2. When pressure is increased, the value of the equilibrium constant remains unaffected.
    3. By increasing the temperature, the forward reaction will be favoured since it is endothermic in nature. Therefore, the value of equilibrium constant will increase. The reaction is endothermic. When the temperature is increased, Kf​ is increased. Hence, Kc = Kf​/Kf​​ is also increased.

question_text: Dihydrogen gas used in Haber's process is produced by reacting methane from natural gas with high temperature steam. The first stage of two stage reaction involves the formation of CO and H 2 . In second stage, CO formed in first stage is reacted with more steam in water gas shift reaction. {tex}CO(g) + {H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons C{O_2}(g) + {H_2}(g){/tex} If a reaction vessel at 400°C is charged with an equimolar mixture of CO and steam such that {tex}p_{co} = p_{H_2 O}{/tex} = 4.0 bar, what will be the partial pressure of H 2 at equilibrium? K p = 0.1 at 400°C.
question_html:
Dihydrogen gas used in Haber's process is produced by reacting methane from natural gas with high temperature steam. The first stage of two stage reaction involves the formation of CO and H2. In second stage, CO formed in first stage is reacted with more steam in water gas shift reaction.
{tex}CO(g) + {H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons C{O_2}(g) + {H_2}(g){/tex}
If a reaction vessel at 400°C is charged with an equimolar mixture of CO and steam such that {tex}p_{co} = p_{H_2 O}{/tex} = 4.0 bar, what will be the partial pressure of H2 at equilibrium? Kp = 0.1 at 400°C.
solution_text: Let the partial pressure of hydrogen (H 2 ) at equilibrium point = p bar {tex}{K_p} = \frac{{p_{CO_2} \times p_{H_2}}}{{p_{CO} \times p_{H_2 O}}}{/tex} or {tex}0.1 = \frac{{(p\;bar) \times (p\;bar)}}{{(4 - p)bar \times (4 - p)bar}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{p^2}}}{{{{(4 - p)}^2}}} = 0.1{/tex} or {tex}\frac{p}{{(4 - p)}} = {(0.1)^{1/2}}{/tex} = 0.316 p = 0.316(4 - p) or p = 1.264 - 0.316 p 1.316 p = 1.264 or {tex}p = \frac{{1.264}}{{1.316}}{/tex} = 0.96 bar
solution_html:
Let the partial pressure of hydrogen (H2) at equilibrium point = p bar

{tex}{K_p} = \frac{{p_{CO_2} \times p_{H_2}}}{{p_{CO} \times p_{H_2 O}}}{/tex} or {tex}0.1 = \frac{{(p\;bar) \times (p\;bar)}}{{(4 - p)bar \times (4 - p)bar}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{{p^2}}}{{{{(4 - p)}^2}}} = 0.1{/tex} or {tex}\frac{p}{{(4 - p)}} = {(0.1)^{1/2}}{/tex} = 0.316
p = 0.316(4 - p) or p = 1.264 - 0.316 p
1.316 p = 1.264 or {tex}p = \frac{{1.264}}{{1.316}}{/tex} = 0.96 bar

question_text: What is meant by conjugate acid base pair? Find the conjugate acid/ base for the following species: HNO 2 , CN - , HClO 4 , F - , OH - , {tex}CO_3^{2-},{/tex} S 2-
question_html:
What is meant by conjugate acid base pair? Find the conjugate acid/ base for the following species:
HNO2, CN-, HClO4, F-, OH-, {tex}CO_3^{2-},{/tex} S2-
solution_text: An acid base pair which differs by a proton only {tex}(HA \rightleftharpoons {A^ - } + {H^ + }){/tex} is known as conjugate acid-base pair. As, Base + H + = conjugate acid. Therefore, Conjugate acid of CN - , OH - , CO 3 2- , F - and S 2- are : HCN, H 2 O, {tex}HCO_3^ - ,{/tex} HF, HS - respectively. As, Acid - H + = conjugate base. Therefore, Conjugate base of HNO 2 , HClO 4 and OH - are NO 2 - , ClO 4 - and O 2- respectively.
solution_html:
An acid base pair which differs by a proton only {tex}(HA \rightleftharpoons {A^ - } + {H^ + }){/tex} is known as conjugate acid-base pair.
As, Base + H+ = conjugate acid. Therefore, Conjugate acid of CN-, OH-, CO32-, F- and S2- are : HCN, H2O, {tex}HCO_3^ - ,{/tex} HF, HS- respectively.
As, Acid - H+ = conjugate base. Therefore, Conjugate base of HNO2, HClO4 and OH- are NO2-, ClO4- and O2- respectively.

Which of the following are Lewis acids?
H2O, BF3, H+ and {tex}NH_4^ + {/tex}
solution_text: Lewis acids have a tendency to accept electrons. Electron deficient or positively charged species have such tendency. Therefore, BF 3 , H + , {tex}NH_4^ + {/tex} ions are Lewis acids.
solution_html: Lewis acids have a tendency to accept electrons. Electron deficient or positively charged species have such tendency. Therefore, BF3, H+, {tex}NH_4^ + {/tex} ions are Lewis acids.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Classify the following species into Lewis acids and Lewis bases and show how these act as Lewis acid/base: OH - F - H + BCl 3
question_html:
Classify the following species into Lewis acids and Lewis bases and show how these act as Lewis acid/base:

  1. OH-
  2. F-
  3. H+
  4. BCl3
solution_text: Hydroxyl ion (OH - ) acts as a Lewis base as it can donate an electron lone pair. Fluoride ion (F - ) acts as a Lewis base as it can donate any one of its four electron lone pairs. A proton (H + ) acts as a Lewis acid as it can accept a lone pair of electrons from bases like hydroxyl ion and fluoride ion. BCl 3 acts as a Lewis acid as it can accept a lone pair of electrons from species like ammonia or amine molecules.
solution_html:
1. Hydroxyl ion (OH-) acts as a Lewis base as it can donate an electron lone pair.
  2. Fluoride ion (F-) acts as a Lewis base as it can donate any one of its four electron lone pairs.
  3. A proton (H+) acts as a Lewis acid as it can accept a lone pair of electrons from bases like hydroxyl ion and fluoride ion.
  4. BCl3 acts as a Lewis acid as it can accept a lone pair of electrons from species like ammonia or amine molecules.

solution_text: If K a is the ioniation constant of weak acis (HA) and K b is the ionization constant of its conjugate (A - ), then K a . K b = K w ; Where, K w = ionic product of water at 25 o C (298K) =10 -14 . Therefore, K a and K b can be calculated by using above formula as follows: For F-, K b = K w /K a {tex} = {10^{ - 14}}/(6.8 \times {10^{ - 4}}) = 1.47 \times {10^{ - 11}} \simeq 1.5 \times {10^{ - 11}}{/tex} . For HCCO - , {tex}{K_b} = {10^{ - 14}}/(1.8 \times {10^{ - 4}}) = 5.6 \times {10^{ - 11}}{/tex} For CN - , {tex}{K_b} = {10^{ - 14}}/(4.8 \times {10^{ - 9}}) = 2.08 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex}

question_text: The ionization constant of phenol is {tex}1.0 \times {10^{ - 10}}{/tex} . What is the concentration of phenolate ion in 0.05 M solution of phenol? What will be its degree of ionization if the solution is also 0.01 M in sodium phenolate?

solution_text: Here, {tex}K_a=\;\frac{\left[C_6H_5O^-\right]\left[H^+\right]}{\left[C_6H_5OH\right]}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{x \times x}}{{0.05 - x}} = 1.0 \times {10^{ - 10}}{/tex} (given) or or {tex}{x^2} = 5 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} or x {tex} = 2.2 \times {10^{ - 6}}M{/tex} In presence of 0.01 C 6 H 5 ONa, suppose y is the amount of phenol dissociated, then at equilibrium {tex}[{C_6}{H_5}OH] = 0.05 - y \simeq 0.05{/tex} {tex}[{C_6}{H_5}{O^ - }] = 0.01 + y \simeq 0.01\;M,[{H^ + }] = y\;M{/tex} {tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{(0.01)(y)}}{{0.05}} = 1.0 \times {10^{ - 10}}{/tex} (given) or {tex}y = 5 \times {10^{ - 10}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \alpha = \frac{y}{c} = \frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} = {10^{ - 8}}{/tex}

Here, {tex}K_a=\;\frac{\left[C_6H_5O^-\right]\left[H^+\right]}{\left[C_6H_5OH\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{x \times x}}{{0.05 - x}} = 1.0 \times {10^{ - 10}}{/tex} (given) or
or {tex}{x^2} = 5 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} or x {tex} = 2.2 \times {10^{ - 6}}M{/tex}
In presence of 0.01 C6H5ONa, suppose y is the amount of phenol dissociated, then at equilibrium
{tex}[{C_6}{H_5}OH] = 0.05 - y \simeq 0.05{/tex}
{tex}[{C_6}{H_5}{O^ - }] = 0.01 + y \simeq 0.01\;M,[{H^ + }] = y\;M{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{(0.01)(y)}}{{0.05}} = 1.0 \times {10^{ - 10}}{/tex} (given) or {tex}y = 5 \times {10^{ - 10}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \alpha = \frac{y}{c} = \frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} = {10^{ - 8}}{/tex}

question_text: The ionization constant of acetic acid is 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 . Calculate the degree of dissociation of acetic acid in its 0.05 M solution. Calculate the concentration of acetate ions in the solution and its pH.
question_html: The ionization constant of acetic acid is 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex}10-5. Calculate the degree of dissociation of acetic acid in its 0.05 M solution. Calculate the concentration of acetate ions in the solution and its pH.
solution_text: For the given acid, {tex}C{H_3}COOH \rightleftharpoons C{H_3}CO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex} C 0 0(initially) {tex}C(1 - \alpha ){/tex} {tex}C\alpha {/tex} {tex}C\alpha {/tex} (At time t) {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{[C{H_3}CO{O^ - }][{H^ + }]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH]}} = \frac{{{{[{H^ + }]}^2}}}{{[C{H_3}COOH]}}{/tex} {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{[C{H_3}CO{O^ - }][{H^ + }]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH]}} = \frac{{{{[{H^ + }]}^2}}}{{[C{H_3}COOH]}}{/tex} or {tex}[{H^ + }] = \sqrt {{K_a}[C{H_3}COOH]} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {(1.74 \times {{10}^{ - 5}})(5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}})} = 9.33 \times {10^{ - 4}}M{/tex} [CH 3 COO - ] = [H + ] {tex} = 9.33 \times {10^{ - 4}}M{/tex} [CH 3 COO - ] = [H + ] = C {tex}\alpha\:{/tex} Therefore, Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha\:=\;\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack}C\;=\;\frac{9.33\times10^{-4}}{0.05}=\;1.86\times10^{-2}{/tex} Now, {tex}pH = - \log (9.33 \times {10^{ - 4}}){/tex} = 4 - 0.9699 = 4 - 0.97 = 3.03
solution_html:
For the given acid, 
{tex}C{H_3}COOH \rightleftharpoons C{H_3}CO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex}

C 0 0(initially)
{tex}C(1 - \alpha ){/tex} {tex}C\alpha {/tex} {tex}C\alpha {/tex} (At time t)

{tex}{K_a} = \frac{{[C{H_3}CO{O^ - }][{H^ + }]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH]}} = \frac{{{{[{H^ + }]}^2}}}{{[C{H_3}COOH]}}{/tex}
{tex}{K_a} = \frac{{[C{H_3}CO{O^ - }][{H^ + }]}}{{[C{H_3}COOH]}} = \frac{{{{[{H^ + }]}^2}}}{{[C{H_3}COOH]}}{/tex}
or {tex}[{H^ + }] = \sqrt {{K_a}[C{H_3}COOH]} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {(1.74 \times {{10}^{ - 5}})(5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}})} = 9.33 \times {10^{ - 4}}M{/tex}
[CH3COO-] = [H+] {tex} = 9.33 \times {10^{ - 4}}M{/tex}
[CH3COO-] = [H+] = C{tex}\alpha\:{/tex}
Therefore, Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha\:=\;\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack}C\;=\;\frac{9.33\times10^{-4}}{0.05}=\;1.86\times10^{-2}{/tex}
Now, {tex}pH = - \log (9.33 \times {10^{ - 4}}){/tex}= 4 - 0.9699 = 4 - 0.97 = 3.03

question_text: It has been found that the pH of a 0.01 M solution of an organic acid is 4.15. Calculate the concentration of the anion, the ionization constant of the acid and its pK a .
question_html: It has been found that the pH of a 0.01 M solution of an organic acid is 4.15. Calculate the concentration of the anion, the ionization constant of the acid and its pKa.
solution_text: For the given organic acid, {tex}HA \rightleftharpoons {H^ + }{/tex} + A - We know that, pH = -log [H + ] or log [H + ] = -4.15 {tex} = \overline 5 .85{/tex} Taking antilog on both side, we get {tex}\therefore [{H^ + }] = 7.09 \times {10^{ - 5}}M = 7.05 \times {10^{ - 5}}M{/tex} [A - ] = [H + ] {tex} = 7.08 \times {10^{ - 5}}M{/tex} Also, {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{[{H^ + }][{A^ - }]}}{{[HA]}} = \frac{{(7.08 \times {{10}^{ - 5}})(7.08 \times {{10}^{ - 5}})}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}} = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} Now, {tex}p{K_a} = - \log {/tex} {tex}{K_a} = - \log (5.0 \times {10^{ - 7}}){/tex} = 7 - 0.699 = 6.301
solution_html:
For the given organic acid, {tex}HA \rightleftharpoons {H^ + }{/tex} + A-
We know that, pH = -log [H+] or log [H+] = -4.15 {tex} = \overline 5 .85{/tex}  
Taking antilog on both side, we get
{tex}\therefore [{H^ + }] = 7.09 \times {10^{ - 5}}M = 7.05 \times {10^{ - 5}}M{/tex}
[A-] = [H+] {tex} = 7.08 \times {10^{ - 5}}M{/tex}  
Also,
{tex}{K_a} = \frac{{[{H^ + }][{A^ - }]}}{{[HA]}} = \frac{{(7.08 \times {{10}^{ - 5}})(7.08 \times {{10}^{ - 5}})}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}} = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} 
Now, {tex}p{K_a} = - \log {/tex}{tex}{K_a} = - \log (5.0 \times {10^{ - 7}}){/tex} = 7 - 0.699 = 6.301

question_text: Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the pH of the following solutions: 0.003 M HCl 0.005 M NaOH 0.002 M HBr 0.002 M KOH
question_html:
Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the pH of the following solutions:

  1. 0.003 M HCl
  2. 0.005 M NaOH
  3. 0.002 M HBr
  4. 0.002 M KOH
solution_text: The pH of 0.003Mof HCl HCl(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H + (aq) +Cl - (aq) {tex}\therefore{/tex} [HCl] = [H + ] = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M; pH = - log [H + ] = -log 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = 3 - 0.4771 = 2.52 The pH of 0.005M of NaOH NaOH(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na + (aq) + OH - (aq) {tex}\therefore{/tex} [NaOH]= [OH - ] = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M; pOH = -log[OH - ] = -log (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 )= 3 - 0.6989 = 2.3010 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH= 14 - 2.3010 = 11.69 The pH of 0.002M of HBr HBr(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H + (aq) + Br - (aq) {tex}\therefore{/tex} [HBr] = [H + ] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M; pH= -log[H + ] = -log (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ) = 3 - 0.3010 = 2.699 {tex}\simeq{/tex} 2.7 The pH of 0.002M of KOH KOH(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} K + (aq) + OH - (aq) {tex}\therefore{/tex} [KOH] = [OH - ] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M; pOH = -log [OH - ] = -log (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ) = 3 - 0.3010 = 2.699 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH= 14 - 2.699 = 11.30
solution_html:
1. The pH of 0.003Mof HCl
    HCl(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H+(aq) +Cl-(aq)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} [HCl] = [H+] = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M; pH = - log [H+]
    = -log 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = 3 - 0.4771 = 2.52
  2. The pH of 0.005M of NaOH
    NaOH(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na+(aq) + OH-(aq)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} [NaOH]= [OH-] = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M; pOH =  -log[OH-] = -log (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3)= 3 - 0.6989 = 2.3010
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH= 14 - 2.3010 = 11.69
  3. The pH of 0.002M of HBr
    HBr(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H+(aq) + Br-(aq)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} [HBr] = [H+] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3M; pH= -log[H+] = -log (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = 3 - 0.3010 = 2.699 {tex}\simeq{/tex} 2.7
  4. The pH of 0.002M of KOH
    KOH(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} K+(aq) + OH-(aq)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} [KOH] = [OH-] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M; pOH = -log [OH-] = -log (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = 3 - 0.3010 = 2.699
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH= 14 - 2.699 = 11.30

question_text: What is the pH of 0.001M aniline solution? The ionization constant of aniline can be taken from Table 6.7. Calculate the degree of ionization of aniline in the solution. Also calculate the ionization constant of the conjugate acid of aniline.
question_html: What is the pH of 0.001M aniline solution? The ionization constant of aniline can be taken from Table 6.7. Calculate the degree of ionization of aniline in the solution. Also calculate the ionization constant of the conjugate acid of aniline.
solution_text: (i) {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2} + {H_2}O \rightleftharpoons {C_6}{H_5}NH_3^ + + O{H^ - }{/tex} {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{[{C_6}{H_5}NH_3^ + ][O{H^ - }]}}{{[{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2}]}} = \frac{{{{[O{H^ - }]}^2}}}{{[{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2}]}}{/tex} {tex}[O{H^ - }] = \sqrt {{K_a}[{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2}]} = \sqrt {(4.27 \times {{10}^{ - 10}})({{10}^{ - 3}})} = 6.534 \times {10^{ - 7}}M{/tex} {tex}pOH = - \log (6.534 \times {10^{ - 7}}) = 7 - 0.8152 = 6.18{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} pH = 14 - 6.18 = 7.82 (ii) Also {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2} + {H_2}O \rightleftharpoons {C_6}{H_5}NH_3^ + + O{H^ - }{/tex} {tex}{K_b} = \frac{{C\alpha \cdot C\alpha }}{{C(1 - \alpha )}} = \frac{{C\alpha }}{{1 - \alpha }} \simeq C{\alpha ^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \alpha = \sqrt {{K_b}/C} = \sqrt {4.27 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}/{{10}^{ - 3}}} = 6.53 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} (iii) pK b + pK b = 14 (for a pair of conjugate acid and base) {tex}p{K_b} = - \log (4.27 \times {10^{ - 10}}){/tex} = 10 - 0.62 = 9.38 {tex}\therefore {/tex} pK b = 14 - 9.38 = 4.62 i.e. -log K a = 4.62 or log K a = -4.62 = 5.38 K a = Antilog {tex}\overline 5 .38 = 2.399 \times {10^{ - 5}} \simeq 2.4 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}
solution_html:
(i) {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2} + {H_2}O \rightleftharpoons {C_6}{H_5}NH_3^ + + O{H^ - }{/tex}
{tex}{K_a} = \frac{{[{C_6}{H_5}NH_3^ + ][O{H^ - }]}}{{[{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2}]}} = \frac{{{{[O{H^ - }]}^2}}}{{[{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2}]}}{/tex}
{tex}[O{H^ - }] = \sqrt {{K_a}[{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2}]} = \sqrt {(4.27 \times {{10}^{ - 10}})({{10}^{ - 3}})} = 6.534 \times {10^{ - 7}}M{/tex}
{tex}pOH = - \log (6.534 \times {10^{ - 7}}) = 7 - 0.8152 = 6.18{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} pH = 14 - 6.18 = 7.82
(ii) Also {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2} + {H_2}O \rightleftharpoons {C_6}{H_5}NH_3^ + + O{H^ - }{/tex}

{tex}{K_b} = \frac{{C\alpha \cdot C\alpha }}{{C(1 - \alpha )}} = \frac{{C\alpha }}{{1 - \alpha }} \simeq C{\alpha ^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \alpha = \sqrt {{K_b}/C} = \sqrt {4.27 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}/{{10}^{ - 3}}} = 6.53 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}
(iii) pKb + pKb = 14 (for a pair of conjugate acid and base)
{tex}p{K_b} = - \log (4.27 \times {10^{ - 10}}){/tex} = 10 - 0.62 = 9.38
{tex}\therefore {/tex} pKb = 14 - 9.38 = 4.62
i.e. -log Ka = 4.62 or log Ka = -4.62 = 5.38
Ka = Antilog {tex}\overline 5 .38 = 2.399 \times {10^{ - 5}} \simeq 2.4 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}

question_html:

  2. 0.1 M HCl?
solution_text: pK a = - log K a = 4.74 or log K a = -4.74 {tex} = \overline 5 .26{/tex} {tex}\therefore {K_a} = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} {tex}\alpha = \sqrt {{K_a}/C} = \sqrt {(1.82 \times {{10}^{ - 5}})/(5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}})} = 1.908 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} In presence of HCl, due to high concentration of H + ion, dissociation equilibrium will shift backward, i.e. dissociation of acetic acid will decrease. In presence of 0.01 MHCl, if x is the amount dissociated, then (0.01 M H + ions are obtained from 0.01 M HCl) {tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{x(0.01)}}{{0.05}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x}{{0.05}} = \frac{{{K_a}}}{{0.01}} = \frac{{1.82 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}} = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex} or {tex}\alpha = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex} {tex}\left( {\because \alpha = \frac{{Amount\;dissociate\;d}}{{Amount\;taken}}} \right){/tex} The degree of ionization is {tex}\alpha = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex} In the presence of 0.1 M HCl, if y is the amount of acetic acid dissociated, then at equilibrium {tex}[C{H_3}COOH] = 0.05 - y \simeq 0.05M{/tex} [CH 3 COO - ] = y, [H + ] {tex} = 0.1M + y \simeq 0.1M{/tex} {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{y(0.1)}}{{0.05}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{y}{{0.05}} = \frac{{{K_2}}}{{0.1}} = \frac{{1.82 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} i.e. {tex}\alpha = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} The degree of ionization is {tex}\alpha = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}

pKa = - log Ka = 4.74
or log Ka = -4.74 {tex} = \overline 5 .26{/tex} {tex}\therefore {K_a} = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha = \sqrt {{K_a}/C} = \sqrt {(1.82 \times {{10}^{ - 5}})/(5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}})} = 1.908 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}
In presence of HCl, due to high concentration of H+ ion, dissociation equilibrium will shift backward, i.e. dissociation of acetic acid will decrease.

  1. In presence of 0.01 MHCl, if x is the amount dissociated, then

    (0.01 M H+ ions are obtained from 0.01 M HCl)
    {tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{x(0.01)}}{{0.05}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x}{{0.05}} = \frac{{{K_a}}}{{0.01}} = \frac{{1.82 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}} = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex}

    {tex}\alpha = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}
    The degree of ionization is  {tex}\alpha = 1.82 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}

    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}[H+] = antilog (–7.8)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}[H+]=1.58{tex}\times{/tex}10-8 M

subject: Chemistry
question_text: If 0.561 g of KOH is dissolved in water to give 200 mL of solution at 298 K, calculate the concentrations of potassium, hydrogen and hydroxyl ions. What is its pH?
question_html: If 0.561 g of KOH is dissolved in water to give 200 mL of solution at 298 K, calculate the concentrations of potassium, hydrogen and hydroxyl ions. What is its pH?
solution_text: Molarity of KOH, M = {tex}\frac{\text { mass of } \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{g}) \times 1000}{\text { molar mass }(\mathrm{KOH}) \times \mathrm{Volume} \text { of solution }(\mathrm{in} \;\mathrm{mL})}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} M = {tex}\frac{0.561 \times 1000}{56 \times 200}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} M = 0.05 mol L -1 We know that, Molar mass of KOH = 39 + 16 + 1 = 56 g mol -1 Reaction: {tex}\mathrm{KOH} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} Now, [K + ] = 0.05 M and [OH - ] = 0.05 M We know that, [H + ][OH] = K w = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} [H + ] = {tex}\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}{0.05}{/tex} = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 M = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 M We know that, pH = -log[H + ] = -log[2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 ] = -0.3010 + 13 = 12.7
solution_html:
Molarity of KOH, M = {tex}\frac{\text { mass of } \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{g}) \times 1000}{\text { molar mass }(\mathrm{KOH}) \times \mathrm{Volume} \text { of solution }(\mathrm{in} \;\mathrm{mL})}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} M = {tex}\frac{0.561 \times 1000}{56 \times 200}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} M = 0.05 mol L-1
We know that, Molar mass of KOH = 39 + 16 + 1 = 56 g mol-1
Reaction:
{tex}\mathrm{KOH} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex}
Now, [K+] = 0.05 M and [OH-] = 0.05 M
We know that, [H+][OH] = Kw = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex}10-14
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}[H+] = {tex}\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}{0.05}{/tex} 
= 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 M
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 M
We know that, pH = -log[H+]
= -log[2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13] 
= -0.3010 + 13
= 12.7

question_text: The solubility of Sr(OH) 2 at 298 K is 19.23 g/L of solution. Calculate the concentration of strontium and hydroxyl ions and the pH of the solution.
question_html: The solubility of Sr(OH)2 at 298 K is 19.23 g/L of solution. Calculate the concentration of strontium and hydroxyl ions and the pH of the solution.
solution_text: Molar mass of Sr(OH) 2 = 87.6 + 34 = 121.6 g mol -1 Molarity of Sr(OH) 2 in moles {tex}{L^{ - 1}} = \frac{{19.23g\;{L^{ - 1}}}}{{121.6g\;mo{l^{ - 1}}}} = 0.1581\;M{/tex} Assuming complete dissociation, {tex}Sr{(OH)_2} \to S{r^{2 + }} + 2O{H^ - }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} [Sr 2+ ] = 0.1581 M, {tex}[O{H^ - }] = 2 \times 0.1581{/tex} = 0.3162 M pOH = -log 0.3162 = 0.5, {tex}\therefore {/tex} pH = 14 - 0.5 = 13.5
solution_html:
Molar mass of Sr(OH)2 = 87.6 + 34 = 121.6 g mol-1
Molarity of Sr(OH)2 in moles {tex}{L^{ - 1}} = \frac{{19.23g\;{L^{ - 1}}}}{{121.6g\;mo{l^{ - 1}}}} = 0.1581\;M{/tex}
Assuming complete dissociation, {tex}Sr{(OH)_2} \to S{r^{2 + }} + 2O{H^ - }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} [Sr2+] = 0.1581 M, {tex}[O{H^ - }] = 2 \times 0.1581{/tex} = 0.3162 M
pOH = -log 0.3162 = 0.5, {tex}\therefore {/tex} pH = 14 - 0.5 = 13.5

question_text: The ionization constant of propanoic acid is {tex}1.32 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} . Calculate the degree of ionization of the acid in its 0.05 M solution and also its pH. What will be its degree of ionization if the solution is 0.01 M HCl also?
question_html: The ionization constant of propanoic acid is {tex}1.32 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}. Calculate the degree of ionization of the acid in its 0.05 M solution and also its pH. What will be its degree of ionization if the solution is 0.01 M HCl also?
solution_text: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}COOH}\limits_{(0.05 - {C_\alpha })} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CO{O^ - }}\limits_{{C_\alpha }} + \mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_{{C_a}} {/tex} Assuming {tex}\alpha {/tex} to be very small, Then by applying the formula; {tex}\alpha = \sqrt {{K_a}/C} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {1.32 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}/0.05} = 1.62 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\lbrack H_3O^+\rbrack\;=\;C\alpha\;=\;0.05\;\times0.016248\;=\;0.0008124\\\lbrack H_3O^+\rbrack\;=8.124\times\;10^{-4}\\pH=\;-\log\;\lbrack H_3O^+\rbrack\;=-\;\log\;(8.124\times\;10^{-4\;})\\pH\;=4-0.9098\;=\;3.0902\;\approx3.09\\\\\end{array}{/tex} In pressure of HCl, equilibrium will sift in the backward direction, i.e. the amount dissociation will be less. {tex}\begin{array}{l}K_a\;=\frac{\left[CH_3CH_2COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3CH_2COOH\right]}\\1.32\times10^{-5}\;=\;\frac{C\alpha\;\times0.01}{(0.05-\;C\alpha)}\;\\\\\end{array}{/tex} Now, {tex}\begin{array}{l}K_a\;=\frac{\left[CH_3CH_2COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3CH_2COOH\right]}\\\frac{\left(1.32\times10^{-5}\right)(0.05-\;C\alpha)}{0.01}\;=\;C\alpha\;\;\;\lbrack\;As(0.05-\;C\alpha)\;\approx0.05\rbrack\\\;\;C\alpha\;\;=\;6.60\times10^{-5}\\degree\;of\;ionization\;,\;\alpha\;\;=\frac{6.60\times10^{-5}}{0.05}=\;1.32\times10^{-3}\\\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}COOH}\limits_{(0.05 - {C_\alpha })} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CO{O^ - }}\limits_{{C_\alpha }} + \mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_{{C_a}} {/tex}

Assuming {tex}\alpha {/tex} to be very small, Then by applying the formula;
{tex}\alpha = \sqrt {{K_a}/C} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {1.32 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}/0.05} = 1.62 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\lbrack H_3O^+\rbrack\;=\;C\alpha\;=\;0.05\;\times0.016248\;=\;0.0008124\\\lbrack H_3O^+\rbrack\;=8.124\times\;10^{-4}\\pH=\;-\log\;\lbrack H_3O^+\rbrack\;=-\;\log\;(8.124\times\;10^{-4\;})\\pH\;=4-0.9098\;=\;3.0902\;\approx3.09\\\\\end{array}{/tex}
In pressure of HCl, equilibrium will sift in the backward direction, i.e. the amount dissociation will be less.

{tex}\begin{array}{l}K_a\;=\frac{\left[CH_3CH_2COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3CH_2COOH\right]}\\1.32\times10^{-5}\;=\;\frac{C\alpha\;\times0.01}{(0.05-\;C\alpha)}\;\\\\\end{array}{/tex}
Now,

{tex}\begin{array}{l}K_a\;=\frac{\left[CH_3CH_2COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3CH_2COOH\right]}\\\frac{\left(1.32\times10^{-5}\right)(0.05-\;C\alpha)}{0.01}\;=\;C\alpha\;\;\;\lbrack\;As(0.05-\;C\alpha)\;\approx0.05\rbrack\\\;\;C\alpha\;\;=\;6.60\times10^{-5}\\degree\;of\;ionization\;,\;\alpha\;\;=\frac{6.60\times10^{-5}}{0.05}=\;1.32\times10^{-3}\\\\\end{array}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The pH of 0.1 M solution of cyanic acid (HCNO) is 2.34. Calculate the ionization constant of the acid and its degree of ionization in the solution.
question_html: The pH of 0.1 M solution of cyanic acid (HCNO) is 2.34. Calculate the ionization constant of the acid and its degree of ionization in the solution.
solution_text: {tex}HCNO \rightleftharpoons {H^ + } + CN{O^ - }{/tex} pH = 2.34 means - log [H + ]= 2.34 or log [H + ] = -2.34. Or, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\log\;\lbrack{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+\rbrack\;=-2.34\;=\overset-3.66\\\lbrack{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+\rbrack\;=\mathrm{antilog}\;\;\overset-3.66\;=4.571\;\times10^{-3}\;\mathrm M\\\mathrm{Or},\;\lbrack{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+\rbrack\;=\;\mathrm{Cα}=\;4.571\;\times10^{-3}\;\mathrm M\\\mathrm{Degree}\;\mathrm{of}\;\mathrm{ionization}\;\mathrm{constant},\;\mathrm\alpha\;=\frac{\;4.571\;\times10^{-3}\;}{0.1}=\;4.571\;\times10^{-2}\;\\\mathrm{Now},\;\mathrm{Ionization}\;\mathrm{constant}\;={\mathrm K}_{\mathrm a\;}=\frac{\left[{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+\right]\left[\mathrm{CNO}^-\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HCNO}\right]}=\frac{\;4.571\;\times10^{-3}\;\times\;4.571\;\times10^{-3}}{0.1}\;=\;2.089\times10^{-4}\\\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}HCNO \rightleftharpoons {H^ + } + CN{O^ - }{/tex}
pH = 2.34 means - log [H+]= 2.34 or log [H+] = -2.34. 

Or, 

{tex}\begin{array}{l}\log\;\lbrack{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+\rbrack\;=-2.34\;=\overset-3.66\\\lbrack{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+\rbrack\;=\mathrm{antilog}\;\;\overset-3.66\;=4.571\;\times10^{-3}\;\mathrm M\\\mathrm{Or},\;\lbrack{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+\rbrack\;=\;\mathrm{Cα}=\;4.571\;\times10^{-3}\;\mathrm M\\\mathrm{Degree}\;\mathrm{of}\;\mathrm{ionization}\;\mathrm{constant},\;\mathrm\alpha\;=\frac{\;4.571\;\times10^{-3}\;}{0.1}=\;4.571\;\times10^{-2}\;\\\mathrm{Now},\;\mathrm{Ionization}\;\mathrm{constant}\;={\mathrm K}_{\mathrm a\;}=\frac{\left[{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+\right]\left[\mathrm{CNO}^-\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HCNO}\right]}=\frac{\;4.571\;\times10^{-3}\;\times\;4.571\;\times10^{-3}}{0.1}\;=\;2.089\times10^{-4}\\\\\end{array}{/tex}

question_text: The ionization constant of nitrous acid is {tex}4.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} . Calculate the pH of 0.04 M sodium nitrite solution and also its degree of hydrolysis.
question_html: The ionization constant of nitrous acid is {tex}4.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}. Calculate the pH of 0.04 M sodium nitrite solution and also its degree of hydrolysis.
solution_text: Given, [NaNO 2 ] = 0.04M , K a of HNO 3 = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 Sodium nitrite is a salt of weak acid, strong base. Hence, {tex}K_h\;=\frac{K_w}{K_a}\;=\;\frac{10^{-14}}{4.5\times10^{-4}}=2.22\times10^{-11}{/tex} {tex}h = \sqrt {{K_h}/c} = \sqrt {2.22 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}/0.04} = \sqrt {5.5 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}} = 2.36 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} {tex}[O{H^ - }] = ch = 0.04 \times 2.36 \times {10^{ - 5}} = 9.44 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} {tex}pOH = - \log (9.44 \times {10^{ - 7}}) = 7 - 0.9750 = 6.03{/tex} {tex}pH = 14 - pOH = 14 - 6.03 = 7.97{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, [NaNO2] = 0.04M , Ka of HNO3 = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex}10-4

Sodium nitrite is a salt of weak acid, strong base. Hence,
{tex}K_h\;=\frac{K_w}{K_a}\;=\;\frac{10^{-14}}{4.5\times10^{-4}}=2.22\times10^{-11}{/tex}
{tex}h = \sqrt {{K_h}/c} = \sqrt {2.22 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}/0.04} = \sqrt {5.5 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}} = 2.36 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}

{tex}[O{H^ - }] = ch = 0.04 \times 2.36 \times {10^{ - 5}} = 9.44 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex}
{tex}pOH = - \log (9.44 \times {10^{ - 7}}) = 7 - 0.9750 = 6.03{/tex}
{tex}pH = 14 - pOH = 14 - 6.03 = 7.97{/tex}

question_text: A 0.02 M solution of pyridinium hydrochloride has pH = 3.44. Calculate the ionization constant of pyridine.
question_html: A 0.02 M solution of pyridinium hydrochloride has pH = 3.44. Calculate the ionization constant of pyridine.
solution_text: pH = 3.44, i.e. {tex}\log [{H^ + }] = - 3.44{/tex} = 4.56 {tex}\therefore [{H^ + }] = 3.63 \times {10^{ - 4}}M{/tex} {tex}C_6H_5N^+HCl^- +H_2O \rightleftharpoons C_6H_5N^+Cl^- + H_3O^+{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}K_a\;=\frac{\left[C_6H_5N^+Cl^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[C_6H_5N^+HCl^-\right]}\end{array}{/tex} pH = 3.44 = - log [H 3 O + ] log [H 3 O + ] = - 3.44 = {tex}\overset-4.56{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [H 3 O + ] = [C 6 H 5 N + Cl - ] = 3.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}Hence,\;K_a\;=\frac{\left[C_6H_5N^+Cl^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[C_6H_5N.HCl\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3.63\;\times10^{-4}\;\times3.63\;\times10^{-4}}{0.02}=\;6.588\;\times10^{-6}{/tex} pK a = - log K a = - log 6.588 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 pK a = 6 + (-0.8187) = 5.18 pK a + pK b = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} pK b = 14-5.18 = 8.82 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - log k b = 8.82 {tex}\log K_b\;=-8.82\;=\overset-9.18{/tex} k b = anti log {tex}\overset-9.18\;=1.5\;\times{/tex} 10 -9 Alternatively, Pyridine hydrochloride is a salt to weak base and strong acid. Hence, C 6 H 5 N.HCl + H 2 O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C 6 H 5 N + HOH - + HCl (Solution is acidic due to hydroysis) {tex}pH=\;7\;-\frac{pK_a}2-\frac{log\;C}2{/tex} {tex}3.44\;=\;7-\frac{pK_a}2-\frac{log\;0.02}2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow pK_b\;=-8.82=\;\overset-9.18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K_b\;=\;anti\log\;\overset-9.18\;=\;1.5\;\times10^{-9}{/tex}
solution_html:
pH = 3.44, i.e. {tex}\log [{H^ + }] = - 3.44{/tex}= 4.56 {tex}\therefore [{H^ + }] = 3.63 \times {10^{ - 4}}M{/tex}
{tex}C_6H_5N^+HCl^- +H_2O \rightleftharpoons C_6H_5N^+Cl^- + H_3O^+{/tex}
{tex}\begin{array}{l}K_a\;=\frac{\left[C_6H_5N^+Cl^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[C_6H_5N^+HCl^-\right]}\end{array}{/tex}
pH = 3.44 = - log [H3O+]
log [H3O+] = - 3.44 = {tex}\overset-4.56{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [H3O+] = [C6H5N+Cl-] = 3.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}Hence,\;K_a\;=\frac{\left[C_6H_5N^+Cl^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[C_6H_5N.HCl\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3.63\;\times10^{-4}\;\times3.63\;\times10^{-4}}{0.02}=\;6.588\;\times10^{-6}{/tex}
pKa = - log Ka = - log 6.588 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
pKa = 6 + (-0.8187) = 5.18
pKa + pKb = 14
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} pKb= 14-5.18 = 8.82
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - log kb= 8.82
{tex}\log K_b\;=-8.82\;=\overset-9.18{/tex}
kb = anti log {tex}\overset-9.18\;=1.5\;\times{/tex} 10-9
Alternatively, Pyridine hydrochloride is a salt to weak base and strong acid. Hence,
C6H5N.HCl + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C6H5N+HOH- + HCl (Solution is acidic due to hydroysis)
{tex}pH=\;7\;-\frac{pK_a}2-\frac{log\;C}2{/tex}
{tex}3.44\;=\;7-\frac{pK_a}2-\frac{log\;0.02}2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow pK_b\;=-8.82=\;\overset-9.18{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow K_b\;=\;anti\log\;\overset-9.18\;=\;1.5\;\times10^{-9}{/tex}

question_html:
Predict if the solutions of the following salts are neutral, acidic or basic:
NaCl, KBr, NaCN, NH4NO3, NaNO2 and KF
solution_text: NaCN, NaNO 2 , KF solutions are basic, as they are salts of strong base, weak acid. NaCl, KBr solutions are neutral, as they are salts of strong acid, strong base. NH 4 NO 3 solution is acidic as it is a salt of strong acid, weak base. This can be sumarized as follows: S.No. Salt Acid Base Hydrolysis Reaction Nature of solution 1. NaCl HCl NaOH H 2 O {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} H + + OH - No Hydrolysis Neutral 2. KBr HBr KOH No Hydrolysis Neutral 3. NaCN HCN NaOH CN - + H 2 O {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} HCN + OH - Basic 4. NH 4 NO 3 HNO 3 NH 4 OH NH 4 + + H 2 O {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} NH 4 OH + H + Acidic 5. NaNO 2 HNO 2 NaOH NO 2 - + H 2 O {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} HNO 2 + OH - Basic 6. KF HF KOH F - + H 2 O {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} HF + OH - Basic
solution_html:
NaCN, NaNO2, KF solutions are basic, as they are salts of strong base, weak acid. NaCl, KBr solutions are neutral, as they are salts of strong acid, strong base. NH4NO3 solution is acidic as it is a salt of strong acid, weak base. This can be sumarized as follows:

S.No. Salt Acid Base Hydrolysis Reaction Nature of solution
1. NaCl HCl NaOH

No Hydrolysis

 Neutral

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The ionization constant of chloroacetic acid is 1.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 . What will be the pH of 0.1 M acid and its 0.1 M sodium salt solution?
question_html: The ionization constant of chloroacetic acid is 1.35{tex}\times{/tex}10-3. What will be the pH of 0.1 M acid and its 0.1 M sodium salt solution?
solution_text: Monochloroacetic acid undergoes dissociation as follows: {tex}ClC{H_2}COOH \rightleftharpoons ClC{H_2}CO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex} Given, K a = 1.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 By Ostwald's dilution law, {tex}[{H^ + }] = \sqrt {{K_a} \times c} = \sqrt {1.35 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 0.1} = 1.16 \times {10^{ - 2}}M{/tex} {tex}pH = - \log (1.16 \times {10^{ - 2}}) = 2 - 0.06 = 1.94{/tex} 0.1M Sodium salt of chloroacetic acid is basic due to hydrolysis. {tex}CH_2ClOO^-+\:H_2O\leftrightarrow CH_2ClCOOH\;+\;OH^-{/tex} For a salt of strong base + weak acid. {tex}pH = - \frac{1}{2}[\log \;{K_w} + \log \;{K_a} - \log \;c]{/tex} {tex}\therefore pH = - \frac{1}{2}[\log {10^{ - 14}} + \log 1.35 \times {10^{ - 13}} - \log \;0.1]{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{1}{2}[ - 14 + ( - 3 + 0.1303) - ( - 1)]{/tex} = 7 + 1.44 - 0.5 = 7.94
solution_html:
Monochloroacetic acid undergoes dissociation as follows: {tex}ClC{H_2}COOH \rightleftharpoons ClC{H_2}CO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex}

Given,  Ka = 1.35{tex}\times{/tex}10-3
By Ostwald's dilution law,
{tex}[{H^ + }] = \sqrt {{K_a} \times c} = \sqrt {1.35 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 0.1} = 1.16 \times {10^{ - 2}}M{/tex}
{tex}pH = - \log (1.16 \times {10^{ - 2}}) = 2 - 0.06 = 1.94{/tex}
0.1M Sodium salt of chloroacetic acid is basic due to hydrolysis.

{tex}CH_2ClOO^-+\:H_2O\leftrightarrow CH_2ClCOOH\;+\;OH^-{/tex}

For a salt of strong base + weak acid.
{tex}pH = - \frac{1}{2}[\log \;{K_w} + \log \;{K_a} - \log \;c]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore pH = - \frac{1}{2}[\log {10^{ - 14}} + \log 1.35 \times {10^{ - 13}} - \log \;0.1]{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac{1}{2}[ - 14 + ( - 3 + 0.1303) - ( - 1)]{/tex} = 7 + 1.44 - 0.5 = 7.94

question_text: Ionic product of water at 310 K is {tex}2.7 \times {10^{ - 14}}{/tex} . What is the pH of neutral water at this temperature?
question_html: Ionic product of water at 310 K is {tex}2.7 \times {10^{ - 14}}{/tex}. What is the pH of neutral water at this temperature?
solution_text: We know that the ionic product of water at given temperature = K w =[H 3 O + ] [OH - ] For water, [H 3 O + ] = [OH - ] ; [H 3 O + ] 2 = [H + ] 2 = K w {tex}[{H^ + }] = \sqrt {{K_w}} = \sqrt {2.7 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}} = 1.643 \times {10^{ - 7}}M{/tex} Hence, pH = -log [H + ] {tex} = - \log (1.643 \times {10^{ - 7}}){/tex} = 7- 0.2156 = 6.78
solution_html:
We know that the ionic product of water at given temperature = Kw =[H3O+] [OH-]

For water, [H3O+] = [OH-] ; [H3O+]2 = [H+]2 = Kw

{tex}[{H^ + }] = \sqrt {{K_w}} = \sqrt {2.7 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}} = 1.643 \times {10^{ - 7}}M{/tex}
Hence, pH = -log [H+] {tex} = - \log (1.643 \times {10^{ - 7}}){/tex} = 7- 0.2156 = 6.78

question_html:
Calculate the pH of the resultant mixtures.

  1. 10 mL of 0.2 M Ca(OH)2 + 25 mL of 0.1 M HCl
  2. 10 mL of 0.01 M H2SO4 + 10 mL of 0.01 M Ca(OH)2

solution_text: 10 mL of 0.2 M {tex}Ca{(OH)_2} = 10 \times 0.2{/tex} millimoles = 2 mmol of Ca(OH) 2 25 mL of 0.1 M HCl {tex} = 25 \times 0.1{/tex} mmol = 2.5 mmol of HCl {tex}Ca{(OH)_2} + 2HCl \to CaC{l_2} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} 0.1 mmol of Ca(OH) 2 reacts with 2 mmol of HCl {tex}\therefore {/tex} 2.5 mmol of HCl will react with 1.25 mmol of Ca(OH) 2 {tex}\therefore {/tex} Ca(OH) 2 left = 2 - 1.25 =0.75 mmol (HCl is the limiting reactant) Total volume of the solution = 10 + 25 = 35 mL {tex}\therefore {/tex} Molarity of Ca(OH) 2 in the mixture solution {tex} = \frac{{0.75}}{{35}}M{/tex} = 0.0214 M {tex}\therefore [O{H^{ - 1}}] = 2 \times 0.0214M{/tex} = 0.0428 M {tex} = 4.28 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}pOH = - \log (4.28 \times {10^{ - 2}}) = 2 - 0.6314 = 1.3686 \cong 1.37{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} pH = 14 - 1.37 = 12.63 10 mL of 0.01M H 2 SOI 4 {tex} = 10 \times 0.01{/tex} mmol = 0.1 mmol 10 mL of 0.01 M Ca(OH) 2 {tex} = 10 \times 0.01{/tex} mmol = 0.1 mmol {tex}Ca{(OH)_2} + {H_2}S{O_4} \to CaS{O_4} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} 1 mole of Ca(OH) 2 reacts with 1 mole of H 2 SO 4 {tex}\therefore {/tex} 1 mmol of Ca(OH) 2 will react completely with 0.1 mmol of H 2 SO 4 . Hence, solution will be neutral with pH = 7.0 10 mL of 0.1 M H 2 SO 4 = 1 mmol 10 mL of 0.1 M KOH = 1 mmol {tex}2KOH + {H_2}S{O_4} \to {K_2}S{O_4} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} 1 millimole of KOH will react with 0.5 mmol of H 2 SO 4 {tex}\therefore {/tex} H 2 SO 4 left = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 mmol Volume of reaction mixture = 10 + 10 = 20 mL {tex}\therefore {/tex} Molarity of H 2 SO 4 in the mixture solution {tex} = \frac{{0.5}}{{20}} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 12}}M{/tex} {tex}[{H^ + }] = 2 \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 5 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}pH = - \log (5 \times {10^{ - 2}}) = 2 - 0.699 = 1.3{/tex}

question_text: The solubility of product constants of Ag 2 CrO 4 and AgBr are {tex}1.1 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} and {tex}5.0 \times {10^{ - 13}}{/tex} respectively. Calculate the ratio of molarities of their saturated solutions.
question_html: The solubility of product constants of Ag2CrO4 and AgBr are {tex}1.1 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} and {tex}5.0 \times {10^{ - 13}}{/tex} respectively. Calculate the ratio of molarities of their saturated solutions.
solution_text: {tex}{\underset s{A}} g_2 CrO_4\rightleftharpoons \underset {2s} 2Ag^+\;+\:\underset s CrO_4^{2-}\;;{/tex} {tex}\underset {(Solubility\;of\;Ag_2CrO_4\;is\;'s'\;mol\;L^{-1}) } {K_{sp}\;=1.1\;\times10^{-12}}{/tex} {tex}K_{sp}\;=\;\lbrack Ag^+\rbrack^2\;\lbrack CrO_4^{2-}\rbrack{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K_{sp}\;=(2s)^2\times s\;=4s^3{/tex} {tex}s^3\;=\frac{K_{sp}}4=\;\frac{1.1\times10^{-12}}4=\;0.275\times\;10^{-12}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow3\;\log s\;=\log\;2.75\times10^{-13}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\;\log\;s\;=-13\;+0.43393\;=\;-12.5607{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\log\;s\;=\frac{\;-12.5607}3\;.1869=\;\overset-5.8131{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow s=\;antilog\;\overset-5.8131\;=6.503\times10^{-5}\;M{/tex} {tex}For,\; \underset s AgBr\rightleftharpoons \underset s Ag^+\;+ \underset s Br^-\;;\;K_{sp}\;{/tex} {tex}= \underset {(Solubility\;of\;AgBr\;is\;'s'\;mol\;L^{-1})} {5.0\;\times10^{-13}}{/tex} {tex}K_{sp}\;=\;\lbrack Ag^+\rbrack\;\lbrack Br\rbrack{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K_{sp}\;=(s)\times s\;=s^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow s^2\;=\sqrt{K_{sp}}=\;\sqrt{5.0\times10^{-13}}=\;7.07\times\;10^{-7}{/tex} Now, Ratio of their solubilities : {tex}\left[ {\frac{{S(A{g_2}Cr{O_4})}}{{S(AgBr)}} = \frac{{6.5 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{7.07 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}} \right] = 91.9{/tex} = 92. Ag 2 CrO 4 is 92 times more soluble than AgBr.

{tex}{\underset s{A}} g_2 CrO_4\rightleftharpoons \underset {2s} 2Ag^+\;+\:\underset s CrO_4^{2-}\;;{/tex} {tex}\underset {(Solubility\;of\;Ag_2CrO_4\;is\;'s'\;mol\;L^{-1}) } {K_{sp}\;=1.1\;\times10^{-12}}{/tex}
{tex}K_{sp}\;=\;\lbrack Ag^+\rbrack^2\;\lbrack CrO_4^{2-}\rbrack{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow K_{sp}\;=(2s)^2\times s\;=4s^3{/tex}
{tex}s^3\;=\frac{K_{sp}}4=\;\frac{1.1\times10^{-12}}4=\;0.275\times\;10^{-12}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow3\;\log s\;=\log\;2.75\times10^{-13}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\;\log\;s\;=-13\;+0.43393\;=\;-12.5607{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\log\;s\;=\frac{\;-12.5607}3\;.1869=\;\overset-5.8131{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow s=\;antilog\;\overset-5.8131\;=6.503\times10^{-5}\;M{/tex}
{tex}For,\; \underset s AgBr\rightleftharpoons \underset s Ag^+\;+ \underset s Br^-\;;\;K_{sp}\;{/tex}{tex}= \underset {(Solubility\;of\;AgBr\;is\;'s'\;mol\;L^{-1})} {5.0\;\times10^{-13}}{/tex}
{tex}K_{sp}\;=\;\lbrack Ag^+\rbrack\;\lbrack Br\rbrack{/tex}

Now, Ratio of their solubilities :
{tex}\left[ {\frac{{S(A{g_2}Cr{O_4})}}{{S(AgBr)}} = \frac{{6.5 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{7.07 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}} \right] = 91.9{/tex} = 92.

question_text: Equal volumes of 0.002 M solutions of sodium iodate and copper chlorate are mixed together. Will it lead to precipitation of copper iodate? For copper iodate, {tex}{K_{sp}} = 7.4 \times {10^{ - 8}}{/tex} .
question_html: Equal volumes of 0.002 M solutions of sodium iodate and copper chlorate are mixed together. Will it lead to precipitation of copper iodate? For copper iodate, {tex}{K_{sp}} = 7.4 \times {10^{ - 8}}{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}\begin{array}{l}2NaIO_3\;+\;Cu(ClO_3)_2\;\rightarrow\;2NaClO_3\;+\;\underset{Copper\;iodate}{Cu(IO_3)_2}\\\end{array}{/tex} Due to mixing of equal volumes, molar concentration of each species is halved, i.e. {tex}[NaI{O_3}] = [IO_3^ - ] = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{2} = {10^{ - 3}}M{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}Cu(ClO_3)_2\;\rightleftharpoons\;Cu^{2+}+\:2IO_3^-\\\end{array}{/tex} Ionic product of Cu(IO 3 ) 2 {tex} = [C{u^{2 + }}]{[IO_3^ - ]^2}{/tex} = (10 -3 ) (10 -3 ) 2 = 10 -9 As ionic product is less than K sp , no precipitation will occur.
solution_html:
{tex}\begin{array}{l}2NaIO_3\;+\;Cu(ClO_3)_2\;\rightarrow\;2NaClO_3\;+\;\underset{Copper\;iodate}{Cu(IO_3)_2}\\\end{array}{/tex}

Due to mixing of equal volumes, molar concentration of each species is halved, i.e. {tex}[NaI{O_3}] = [IO_3^ - ] = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{2} = {10^{ - 3}}M{/tex}
{tex}\begin{array}{l}Cu(ClO_3)_2\;\rightleftharpoons\;Cu^{2+}+\:2IO_3^-\\\end{array}{/tex}
Ionic product of Cu(IO3)2 {tex} = [C{u^{2 + }}]{[IO_3^ - ]^2}{/tex} = (10-3) (10-3)2 = 10-9
As ionic product is less than Ksp , no precipitation will occur.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The ionization constant of benzoic acid is {tex}6.46 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} and K sp for silver benzoate is {tex}2.5 \times {10^{ - 13}}{/tex} . How many times is silver benzoate more soluble in a buffer of pH 3.19 compared to its solubility in pure water?
question_html: The ionization constant of benzoic acid is {tex}6.46 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} and Ksp for silver benzoate is {tex}2.5 \times {10^{ - 13}}{/tex}. How many times is silver benzoate more soluble in a buffer of pH 3.19 compared to its solubility in pure water?
solution_text: {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOAg \to {C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - } + A{g^ + }{/tex} Solubility of water. Suppose solubility in water = x mol L -1 then [C 6 H 5 COO - ] = [Ag + ] = x mol L -1 x 2 = K sp or {tex}x = \sqrt {{K_{sp}}} = \sqrt {2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 13}}} {/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}=5\times10^{-7}\;mol\;L^{-1}\\\end{array}{/tex} Solubility in buffer of pH = 3.19 pH = 3.19 means {tex} - \log [{H^ + }]{/tex} = 3.19 or {tex}\log [{H^ + }] = - 3.19 = \overline 4 .81{/tex} or {tex}[{H^ + }] = 6.457 \times {10^{ - 4}}M{/tex} C 6 H 5 COO - ions now combine with the H + ions to form benzoic acid but [H + ] remains almost constant because we have buffer solution. Now {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOH \rightleftharpoons {C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{[{C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - }][{H^ + }]}}{{[{C_6}{H_5}COOH]}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{[{C_6}{H_5}COOH]}}{{[{C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - }]}} = \frac{{[{H^ + }]}}{{{K_a}}} = \frac{{6.457 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{6.46 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = 10{/tex} .... (i) Suppose solubility in the buffer solution is 'y' mol L -1 . Then as most of the benzoate ions are converted into benzoic acid molecules (which remain almost ionized), we have y = [Ag + ] = [C 6 H 5 COO - ] + [C 6 H 5 COOH] = [C 6 H 5 COO - ] + 10[C 6 H 5 COO - ] = 11[C 6 H 5 COO - ] using equation (i) {tex}\therefore [{C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - }] = \frac{y}{{11}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {K_{sp}} = [{C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - }][A{g^ + }]{/tex} {tex}2.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} = \frac{y}{{11}} \times y{/tex} or {tex}{y^2} = 2.75 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} or {tex}y = 1.66 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x} = \frac{{1.66 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{/tex} = 3.32 Note that in case of salts of weak acids, the solubility is more in the acidic solution than in water. The reason, in general, may be explained as follows: Taking example of C 6 H 5 COOAg, we have {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOAg \rightleftharpoons {C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - } + A{g^ + }{/tex} In acidic solution, the anions (C 6 H 5 COO - in the present case) undergo protonation in presence of acid. Thus, C 6 H 5 COO - ions are removed. Hence, equilibrium shifts forward producing more Ag + ions. Alternatively, as C 6 H 5 COO - ions are removed, Q sp decreases. In order to maintain solubility product equilibrium (Q sp = K sp ), Ag + ion concentration must increase. Hence, solubility is more.
solution_html:
{tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOAg \to {C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - } + A{g^ + }{/tex}
Solubility of water. Suppose solubility in water = x mol L-1 then
[C6H5COO-] = [Ag+] = x mol L-1
x2 = Ksp or {tex}x = \sqrt {{K_{sp}}} = \sqrt {2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 13}}} {/tex}
{tex}\begin{array}{l}=5\times10^{-7}\;mol\;L^{-1}\\\end{array}{/tex}
Solubility in buffer of pH = 3.19
pH = 3.19 means {tex} - \log [{H^ + }]{/tex} = 3.19
or {tex}\log [{H^ + }] = - 3.19 = \overline 4 .81{/tex} or {tex}[{H^ + }] = 6.457 \times {10^{ - 4}}M{/tex}
C6H5COO- ions now combine with the H+ ions to form benzoic acid but [H+] remains almost constant because we have buffer solution. Now
{tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOH \rightleftharpoons {C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {K_a} = \frac{{[{C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - }][{H^ + }]}}{{[{C_6}{H_5}COOH]}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{[{C_6}{H_5}COOH]}}{{[{C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - }]}} = \frac{{[{H^ + }]}}{{{K_a}}} = \frac{{6.457 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{6.46 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = 10{/tex} .... (i)
Suppose solubility in the buffer solution is 'y' mol L-1. Then as most of the benzoate ions are converted into benzoic acid molecules (which remain almost ionized), we have
y = [Ag+] = [C6H5COO-] + [C6H5COOH] = [C6H5COO-] + 10[C6H5COO-] = 11[C6H5COO-] using equation (i)
{tex}\therefore [{C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - }] = \frac{y}{{11}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {K_{sp}} = [{C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - }][A{g^ + }]{/tex}
{tex}2.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} = \frac{y}{{11}} \times y{/tex} or {tex}{y^2} = 2.75 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} or {tex}y = 1.66 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{y}{x} = \frac{{1.66 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{/tex}= 3.32
Note that in case of salts of weak acids, the solubility is more in the acidic solution than in water. The reason, in general, may be explained as follows: Taking example of C6H5COOAg, we have
{tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOAg \rightleftharpoons {C_6}{H_5}CO{O^ - } + A{g^ + }{/tex}
In acidic solution, the anions (C6H5COO- in the present case) undergo protonation in presence of acid. Thus, C6H5COO- ions are removed. Hence, equilibrium shifts forward producing more Ag+ ions. Alternatively, as C6H5COO- ions are removed, Qsp decreases. In order to maintain solubility product equilibrium (Qsp = Ksp), Ag+ ion concentration must increase. Hence, solubility is more.

question_text: What is the maximum concentration of equimolar solutions of ferrous sulphate and sodium sulphide so that when mixed in equal volumes, there is no precipitation of iron sulphide? (For iron sulphide, {tex}{K_{sp}} = 6.3 \times {10^{ - 18}}){/tex} .
question_html: What is the maximum concentration of equimolar solutions of ferrous sulphate and sodium sulphide so that when mixed in equal volumes, there is no precipitation of iron sulphide? (For iron sulphide, {tex}{K_{sp}} = 6.3 \times {10^{ - 18}}){/tex}.
solution_text: Suppose the concentration of each of FeSO 4 and Na 2 S is x mol L -1 . Then after mixing equal volumes, [FeSO 4 ] = [Na 2 S] {tex} = \frac{x}{2}M{/tex} , i.e. {tex}[F{e^{2 + }}] = [{S^{2 - }}] = \frac{x}{2}M{/tex} K sp for FeS = [Fe 2+ ] [S 2- ] i.e. {tex}6.3 \times {10^{ - 18}} = \frac{x}{2} \times \frac{x}{2}{/tex} [Since, equimolar solutions are to be mixed, hence on mixing concentration is halved.] {tex}{x^2} = 25.2 \times {10^{ - 18}},{/tex} or {tex}x = 5.02 \times {10^{ - 9}}M{/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose the concentration of each of FeSO4 and Na2S is x mol L-1. Then after mixing equal volumes,
[FeSO4] = [Na2S] {tex} = \frac{x}{2}M{/tex}, i.e. {tex}[F{e^{2 + }}] = [{S^{2 - }}] = \frac{x}{2}M{/tex}
Ksp for FeS = [Fe2+] [S2-] i.e. {tex}6.3 \times {10^{ - 18}} = \frac{x}{2} \times \frac{x}{2}{/tex}         [Since, equimolar solutions are to be mixed, hence on mixing concentration is halved.]
{tex}{x^2} = 25.2 \times {10^{ - 18}},{/tex} or {tex}x = 5.02 \times {10^{ - 9}}M{/tex}

question_text: What is the minimum volume of water required to dissolve 1g of calcium sulphate at 298 K. For calcium sulphate, {tex}{K_{sp}} = 9.1 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex} .
question_html: What is the minimum volume of water required to dissolve 1g of calcium sulphate at 298 K. For calcium sulphate, {tex}{K_{sp}} = 9.1 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}CaS{O_4}(s) \rightleftharpoons C{a^2}(aq) + SO_4^{2 - }(aq){/tex} ; K sp = {tex}\begin{array}{l}9.1\;\times10^{-6}\;M\\\end{array}{/tex} If s is the solubility of CaSO 4 in moles L -1 , the {tex}\begin{array}{l}\sqrt{K_{sp}}\;=\;\sqrt{9.1\;\times10^{-6}\;M}\;=\;\lbrack Ca^{2+}\;\rbrack\lbrack SO_4^{2-}\rbrack\;=\;s.s\;=s^2\\s=\;3.017\times10^{-3}\\\\\end{array}{/tex} or CaSO 4 solublity = {tex} = 3.02 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 136g{L^{ - 1}} = 0.411g{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} (molar mass of CaSO 4 = 136 g mol -1 ) Thus, for dissolving 0.411 g, water required = 1L {tex}\therefore {/tex} For dissolving 1g, water required {tex} = \frac{1}{{0.411}}L = 2.43L{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}CaS{O_4}(s) \rightleftharpoons C{a^2}(aq) + SO_4^{2 - }(aq){/tex} ; Ksp = {tex}\begin{array}{l}9.1\;\times10^{-6}\;M\\\end{array}{/tex}
If s is the solubility of CaSO4 in moles L-1, the

 {tex}\begin{array}{l}\sqrt{K_{sp}}\;=\;\sqrt{9.1\;\times10^{-6}\;M}\;=\;\lbrack Ca^{2+}\;\rbrack\lbrack SO_4^{2-}\rbrack\;=\;s.s\;=s^2\\s=\;3.017\times10^{-3}\\\\\end{array}{/tex}
or CaSO4 solublity ={tex} = 3.02 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 136g{L^{ - 1}} = 0.411g{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}                   (molar mass of CaSO4 = 136 g mol-1)
Thus, for dissolving 0.411 g, water required = 1L
{tex}\therefore {/tex} For dissolving 1g, water required {tex} = \frac{1}{{0.411}}L = 2.43L{/tex}

question_text: The concentration of sulphide ion in 0.1M HCl solution saturated with hydrogen sulphide is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 M. If 10 mL of this is added to 5 mL of 0.04 M solution of the following: FeSO 4 , MnCl 2 , ZnCl 2 and CdCl 2 . In which of these solutions precipitation will take place?
question_html: The concentration of sulphide ion in 0.1M HCl solution saturated with hydrogen sulphide is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 M. If 10 mL of this is added to 5 mL of 0.04 M solution of the following: FeSO4, MnCl2, ZnCl2 and CdCl2. In which of these solutions precipitation will take place?
solution_text: Precipitation will take place in the solution for which ionic product is greater than solubility product. As 10 mL of a solution containing S 2- ion is mixed with 5 mL of metal salt solution, after mixing Total volume after mixing is 10 + 5 = 15 mL. {tex}\left[ {{S^{2 - }}} \right] = 1.0 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times \frac{{10}}{{15}} = 6.67 \times {10^{ - 20}}{/tex} {tex}[F{e^{2 + }}] = [M{n^{2 + }}] = [Z{n^{2 + }}] = [C{d^{2 + }}]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{5}{{15}} \times 0.04 = 1.33 \times {10^{ - 2}}M{/tex} As the ionic product is greater than solubility product of ZnS and CdS. So, these (CdCl 2 and ZnCl 2 ) are precipitated as CdS and ZnS.
